Thank you for re-writing this!

The test suite that should be used is my fork - https://github.com/uiri/toml-test
The BurntSushi test suite is missing approximately 30 tests.

The harness script has been relocated to `tests/decoding_test.py`.

There used to be a separate harness for python3, however that has been relocated to `tests/example_test.py`. I believe this script can be safely removed.

This toml library is intended to be compatible with both python2 and python3. The test suite should therefore be run against both versions. Whichever python2 and python3 one has installed locally ought to be fine - continuous integration takes care of a more thorough round of testing against various releases of each version. The `decoding_test.sh` is intended for Travis while `decoding_test2.sh` and `decoding_test3.sh` is intended for local developer use.

I'll also note that `print()` is valid in both Python 2 and Python 3. `print x` is valid in Python 2 but invalid in Python 3.

How would you feel about renaming this file to CONTRIBUTING? Is there any other information which you think might be helpful to new contributors? I have been working on this code base on and off for so long that it is a little bit difficult for me to see the forest for the trees without a bit of prodding.

I would prefer the file not have the `md` extension. I think the filename looks cleaner without an extension. The license file does not have an extension either and the README only has an extension so for pretty printing on PyPI.

Hi Ted,

Apologies for the late response.

Thank you for your documentation and distribution efforts.

I think that LICENSE should stay without a file extension. That seems to be the norm in open source projects (e.g. CPython itself at github.com/python/cpython). File extensions are an MS-DOS/Windows convention so my UNIX bias is definitely showing through here.

I am a little concerned that GitHub won't recognize CONTRIBUTING.rst while it would recognize CONTRIBUTING or CONTRIBUTING.md. I do recognize that mixing both reStructuredText and markdown in the repository is a little awkward.

Everything else looks good; just those two nits.

But it does improve the performance.

So perhaps I should give you a bit of context. The purpose of this MR is to allow developers override the tag parsing behavior, with their own. Banks don't always adhere to MT940/other standards (which is our particular case, out of 6 banks, 1 doesn't do this). Ideally, we should take the matter back to the bank, but you're probably aware how slow those changes are going to happen (if at all).

It also defaults to the already existing tag regexes, so simply using the `mt940.models.Transactions` without `tags=...` will also work. Let me know what you think, thanks!

Indeed. Interoperable API is more interesting than interoperable runtime. Also, if we think about the bigger picture here. Applications are not just written in Python or the same Python frameworks.

You can have one application/process that is written in Curio and fully harvest the power of Curio or Trio or whatever XXXio. You can also have another application in another language in another machine possibly. Their communication is better down through common networking/sys interface such as sockets/files.

Even if you feel socket/TCP is too low level, you can use protocols such h2 and data serialization format such as protobuf to communicate between process boundaries.

@dangerousfeng 
I read your code(have not run yet) and find some problems:

1. StrictRedis is a client which can hold multi connections, naming it as `self.conn` seems not appropriate
2. The pipe is shared in the object(if instantiated), which seems meaningless, all operation of pipeline is packaged in your functions and not shared.(ignore this if there are other usage of pipeline between two functions)
3. `self.pipe.exists`, `self.pipe.hget` and `self.pipe.execute` is assumed to run in the contextmanager generated by `async with self.conn.pipeline(transaction=True) as pipe` some clean job will be executed after the contextmanager end. And now it won't be executed in your code.
4. since all of your function only contains one command like `hget` and `exists`, pipeline and transaction is not needed. Pipeline is used to send multi commands in one network communication to reduce time spent on IO.

For now, i can only see these problems, if you still have other problems, please let me know.

Changes Unknown when pulling **8aa9a261e6168a66a21c7498797a5eeaef9e9bfa on issue_81** into ** on master**.

The problem here are the following two entries in `_domain_model.text`:

- [Line 41](https://github.com/c-w/Gutenberg/blob/307bb3d2cdaa8a0142342b7e82c5e81988510de7/gutenberg/_domain_model/text.py#L41): "SERVICE THAT CHARGES FOR DOWNLOAD"
-  [Line 72](https://github.com/c-w/Gutenberg/blob/307bb3d2cdaa8a0142342b7e82c5e81988510de7/gutenberg/_domain_model/text.py#L72): "<<THIS ELECTRONIC VERSION OF"

The implementation of `strip_headers` clears the output text whenever it encounters a line indicating the end of the licence header (such as "SERVICE THAT CHARGES FOR DOWNLOAD"). Due to the interspersed licence texts in the Shakespeare file, this means that the output is cleared too often.

While I'm working on a full fix, please find below a partial fix that correctly removes the legalese header and footer (but doesn't handle the interspersed legal disclaimers).

```patch
diff --git a/gutenberg/_domain_model/text.py b/gutenberg/_domain_model/text.py
index 9439c93..f2db8b8 100644
--- a/gutenberg/_domain_model/text.py
+++ b/gutenberg/_domain_model/text.py
@@ -38,10 +38,10 @@ TEXT_START_MARKERS = frozenset((u(_) for _ in (
     "l'authorization <C3><A0> les utilizer pour preparer ce texte.",
     "of the etext through OCR.",
     "*****These eBooks Were Prepared By Thousands of Volunteers!*****",
-    "SERVICE THAT CHARGES FOR DOWNLOAD",
     "We need your donations more than ever!",
     " *** START OF THIS PROJECT GUTENBERG",
     "****     SMALL PRINT!",
+    '["Small Print" V.',
 )))
 
 
@@ -69,7 +69,6 @@ TEXT_END_MARKERS = frozenset((u(_) for _ in (
     "Ce document fut pr<C3><A9>sent<C3><A9> en lecture",
     "More information about this book is at the top of this file.",
     "We need your donations more than ever!",
-    "<<THIS ELECTRONIC VERSION OF",
     "END OF PROJECT GUTENBERG",
     " End of the Project Gutenberg",
     " *** END OF THIS PROJECT GUTENBERG",
```

But adding it to the `EnsembleVoteClassifier` would also be a nice enhancement (and the results may be a bit different from setting the class weights in the initial classifiers). I think it would not be too complicated to add. It currently uses the `weights` as an optional weighting of the individual classifiers, i.e., 

```
            maj = np.apply_along_axis(lambda x:
                                      np.argmax(np.bincount(x,
                                                weights=self.weights)),
                                      axis=1,
                                      arr=predictions)
```

As an alternative to weight on class labels, one could just multiply the predictions I guess. E.g.,

if we have 3 classifiers that predict the three samples as `[0, 1, 1, 0, 1]` and you say that class 0 should be weighted twice as much as class 1, then it would become `[0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1]` from which you then can select the majority class label as a prediction.

Sorry, my first response is super long! 

> But the `filler_feature_dict` would only need to be provided if `feature_index` is not manually provided (i.e., not `None`), right?

The `filler_feature_dict` will always need to be provided if there are more than 2 training features. Say that someone has trained a classifier on the full iris data set (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width) and wants to plot the decision region for sepal length vs. sepal width. Below are 10 random samples from the iris dataset
```
[[ 4.6,  3.4,  1.4,  0.3],
  [ 4.6,  3.1,  1.5,  0.2],
  [ 5.7,  2.5,  5. ,  2. ],
  [ 4.8,  3. ,  1.4,  0.1],
  [ 4.8,  3.4,  1.9,  0.2],
  [ 7.2,  3. ,  5.8,  1.6],
  [ 5. ,  3. ,  1.6,  0.2],
  [ 6.7,  2.5,  5.8,  1.8],
  [ 6.4,  2.8,  5.6,  2.1],
  [ 4.8,  3. ,  1.4,  0.3]]
```

When the `predict` method for the classifier is called, there need to be four training features. That is, the sepal width and sepal length columns alone (shown below) will raise an error because the classifier was trained with 4 features. 

```
[[ 4.6,  3.4],
 [ 4.6,  3.1],
 [ 5.7,  2.5],
 [ 4.8,  3. ],
 [ 4.8,  3.4],
 [ 7.2,  3. ],
 [ 5. ,  3. ],
 [ 6.7,  2.5],
 [ 6.4,  2.8],
 [ 4.8,  3. ]]
```

The user needs to specify values to be filled in for the other features (in this case the petal length and petal width). Say, they specify a petal length filler value of 1.2 and petal width filler value of 0.3. Then the new feature array would be something like

```
[[ 4.6,  3.4,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 4.6,  3.1,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 5.7,  2.5,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 4.8,  3. ,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 4.8,  3.4,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 7.2,  3. ,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 5. ,  3. ,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 6.7,  2.5,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 6.4,  2.8,  1.2,  0.3],
 [ 4.8,  3. ,  1.2,  0.3]]
```

This example, plotting the sepal length and width decision region for a petal length of 1.2 and a petal width of 0.3, would be implemented using the following code. 

```python
plot_decision_regions(X_iris, y_iris, clf,
                        feature_index=(0, 1),
                        filler_feature_dict={2: 1.2, 3:0.3})
```

That was pretty long-winded, hopefully my explanation makes some sense


> Hm, not sure, but is there a reason why you would use different filler values for different feature columns? 

I think that the filler values will change what the decision regions look like. In the above example, if instead of choosing a petal length of 1.2 and petal width of 0.3 the user chose 1.2 for both the petal length and petal width, one would expect the decision boundaries to look different from the 1.2 and 0.3 case. While a single filler value can be used when there are 3 training features, for more than 3 features multiple filler values should be possible. 

Although, you bring up a good point, we could build into `plot_decision_regions` the syntax `filler_feature_dict=<number>` if the user does indeed want to use the same value for all the filler feature columns.

Thanks I looked through the 6.0 release notes. 

No what they added is the option of a dilated convolution which is not the same basically it looks like they changed the purpose of the scaling parameter. But you could add dilate factor in the convolution parameter. Many other Deep learning packages have this as an optional parameter already. I've seen it in Caffe and MatConvNet. Besides for convolution transpose you can already use the CuDNN calls just the order of them is reversed.

Scanning further through the release notes. The interesting bits are probably the new RNN stuff, the reduce Tensor, ELU Activaton and maybe the Int8 type. But I guess if you want to add support for that you would have to rewrite tensor class to be of a template element type which looks like it might be a lot of work.

I had a similar problem in Ubuntu 18.04. Here are the steps I used to solve it:

1. cd to the dLib build directory
2. rm -rf *
3. cmake … (this fails on the CUDA test file so no CUDA support)
4. rm -rf *
5. cmake … -DCUDA_HOST_COMPILER=/usr/bin/gcc-6 (Now I have CUDA support)
6. cmake --build . --config Release
7. sudo ldconfig
8. cd …
9. python3 setup.py install (fails on CUDA test program, no cuda support)
10. I forgot to enable my python env
11. workon cv
12. python3 setup.py install (fails on Cuda Test program, no CUDA support)
13. python3 setup.py --compiler-flags “-DCUDA_HOST_COMPILER=usr/bin/gcc-6” install (no failures, but does not say CUDA support so I am assuming I have it)

I ran into this a while ago. I think the problem is that one of the Windows backends, EncryptedKeyring has a fixed priority of .8 regardless of whether or not it's supported.

This priority is higher than the file based keyrings. If, for whatever reason, you can't use any platform specific keyring in *NIX then the Windows keyring will be used first.

[See here](https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/src/3827c087ed2710a912a4e202c49e3639472fbcbd/keyring/backends/Windows.py?at=default#cl-38)

```Python
class OrderedWeights(bt.Algo):
    def __init__(self, weights):
        self.target_weights = weights

mom1 = bt.Strategy('mom1', [RunOnDataFrameDates(group1),
```

     >>> def check_config_mode(self, check_string=')#'):

by default, the backend uses multi-threading (equivalent to ``backend='threading'``), but you can also run on multi-processing (``backend='multiprocessing'``). 

When you run on multiprocessing, each ``n_job`` is a separate process. When launching these, Python corrupts the thread state in child processes, so that each think more threads are available than what is the case. Scikit-learn estimators are single-threaded, so this won't be an issue. Other packages aren't, in which case each ``n_job`` will be waiting for another to finish, causing a deadlock. 

You won't get an error when this happens, you'll just notice that nothing is happening. If you instead run on multi-threading, this issues doesn't arise. Here's some [more](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/faq.html#why-do-i-sometime-get-a-crash-freeze-with-n-jobs-1-under-osx-or-linux) info on this issue.

I believe `open_files()` does not work properly on any Windows version. The C APIs provided by Windows are extremely low level, undocumented and apparently just broken (https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/arch/windows/process_handles.c), and AFAIK there's nothing out there which is able to do this reliably unless a Windows driver or service (not sure) is involved, which is a no-no for a Python lib. Running the Python process with the highest user privileges (NT/SYSTEM) may possibly alleviate the issue but I believe even in that case `open_files()` will keep being reliable. The only good news about it is that at some point we managed to solve the "hanging" issue by introducing a thread, but the fact that certain perfectly legitimate files are being skipped is still not solved. @wj32 (Process Hacker author) is way more expert than me on this. I am not sure whether PH is able to do this reliably without using a driver/service.

```python
{'chat': {'id': -11111111, 'title': 'Moby', 'type': 'group'},
 'date': 1467664036,
 'from': {'first_name': 'Nick', 'id': 22222222, 'last_name': 'Lee'},
 'message_id': 12019,
 'new_chat_member': {'first_name': 'Chef',
                     'id': 99999999,
                     'username': 'KitchenBot'},
 'new_chat_participant': {'first_name': 'Chef',
                          'id': 99999999,
                          'username': 'KitchenBot'}}
```
The field `new_chat_member` is the current standard. However, to keep backward compatibility, Telegram keeps the old field `new_chat_participant` in the message. They are identical, as you can see.

Telepot's namedtuples follow the current standard, so it has the field `new_chat_member`. As you try to convert a message dictionary into a namedtuple (`telepot.namedtuple.Message(**msg)`), if there is any unexpected field, the above warning will be issued.

So, there is really nothing wrong. I hope you can tolerate the warning.

I solved this problem by creating a client class that tries to ping before each command, and if it fails, reestablishes the connection.  By pinging first, we avoid bad sideeffects from interrupted commands.

I've been using it for a few days, and it seems stable.

    import mpd
    import socket

    class PersistentMPDClient(mpd.MPDClient):
        def __init__(self, socket=None, host=None, port=None):
            super(PersistentMPDClient, self).__init__()
            self.socket = socket
            self.host   = host
            self.port   = port
            # define as empty so intercepted calls prior to getting the actual list 
            # don't fail
            self.command_list = []

            self.do_connect()
            # get list of available commands from client
            self.command_list = self.commands()

            # commands not to intercept
            self.command_blacklist = ['ping']

            # wrap all valid MPDClient functions
            # in a ping-connection-retry wrapper
            for cmd in self.command_list:
                if cmd not in self.command_blacklist:
                    if hasattr(super(PersistentMPDClient, self), cmd):
                        super_fun = super(PersistentMPDClient, self).__getattribute__(cmd)
                        new_fun = self.try_cmd(super_fun)
                        print("Setting interceptor for {}".format(cmd))
                        setattr(self, cmd, new_fun)
                    else:
                        print("Attr {} not available!".format(cmd))

        # create a wrapper for a function (such as an MPDClient
        # member function) that will verify a connection (and
        # reconnect if necessary) before executing that function.
        # functions wrapped in this way should always succeed
        # (if the server is up)
        # we ping first because we don't want to retry the same
        # function if there's a failure, we want to use the noop
        # to check connectivity
        def try_cmd(self, cmd_fun):
            def fun(*pargs, **kwargs):
                try:
    #                print("Attemping to ping...")
                    self.ping()
                except (mpd.ConnectionError, OSError) as e:
    #                print("lost connection.")
    #                print("trying to reconnect.")
                    self.do_connect()
                return cmd_fun(*pargs, **kwargs)
            return fun

        # needs a name that does not collide with parent connect() function
        def do_connect(self):
            try:
                try:
    #                print("Attempting to disconnect.")
                    self.disconnect()
                # if it's a TCP connection, we'll get a socket error 
                # if we try to disconnect when the connection is lost
                except mpd.ConnectionError as e:
    #                print("Disconnect failed, so what?")
                    pass
                # if it's a socket connection, we'll get a BrokenPipeError
                # if we try to disconnect when the connection is lost
                # but we have to retry the disconnect, because we'll get 
                # an "Already connected" error if we don't.
                # the second one should succeed.
                except BrokenPipeError as e:
    #                print("Pipe closed, retrying disconnect.")
                    try:
    #                    print("Retrying disconnect.")
                        self.disconnect()
                     except Exception as e:
                        print("Second disconnect failed, yikes.")
                        print(e)
                        pass
                if self.socket:
    #                print("Connecting to {}".format(self.socket))
                    self.connect(self.socket, None)
                else:
    #                print("Connecting to {}:{}".format(self.host, self.port))
                    self.connect(self.host, self.port)
             except socket.error as e:
                print("Connection refused.")
    #            print(e)

First, you've got a syntax error in the command.   Try this:

`python ofxtools\Client.py -u ***** --org chase -C **** --bankid 111000614 https://ofx.chase.com `

See, [server] is a positional argument; you don't actually type that out.  If you did type out a long-form argument, you need to prefix it with two dashes (not one).  argparse is reading -server as -s erver, i.e. you're trying to set DTSTART='erver', which is of course a syntax error (DTSTART and DTEND are of the form YYYYMMDD).  Just put the naked URL at the end of the command as a positional argument.

Second, you probably only have ofxtools installed as a python3 module, not also as a python2 module.  Try running python2 setup.py and see where that gets you.

The [environment markers from PEP-508](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0508/#environment-markers) are used for conditional install of `ordereddict` in `install_requires`:

    ordereddict>=1.1; python_version < "2.7"

However, it seems the new syntax is not properly supported in older versions of `setuptools`, and that the environment markers are being stripped when building a wheel in `setuptools < 36.2.1`.

Resolution is to use the old syntax, for time being: `':python_version=="2.7"': [...]`.

For discussion, see: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/troubleshooting/issues/1.

* I think that the ["search" event](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#search) should always be enabled, since it's the developer triggering it on the table cell and not the user.
* Within the `filter_searchTrigger`, let's use key names like 'enter', 'tab', etc, instead of the key code number. They can be added to the [`$.tablesorter.keyCodes` definition](https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/blob/master/js/widgets/widget-filter.js#L19-L25). * `filter_searchTrigger` should have a default of `['blur', 'enter']`, not `false` because that would imply that there is no search trigger.

The three user-response cases: adult, user/pass and PIL not-installed; can all be avoided by using personal.ini settings.

- You can already skip the adult check by setting `is_adult` in personal.ini, as I see you've found.
- User/pass can be saved per site in personal.ini.
- There are three ways already to avoid the prompt about PIL: don't turn on `include_images` (it defaults to off in CLI), install PIL, or set `no_image_processing` in personal.ini.

And you can set INI options from command-line if you really want to do it from CLI with `-o`.

But while my first inclination was to say there's no need, I can see where it would be a frustration to figure those out as they crop up.

I think I'd rather see:
- No direct adult option. `-o is_adult:true` is equivalent.
- Set the default value for `options.interactive` using `sys.stdin.isatty() and sys.stdout.isatty()`.  That way it will default to non-interactive while scripted/piped (on either input or output) and interactive when attached to a terminal while still allowing user override.
- Add some comments on the `print 'y'` `print 'n ...'` lines about faking appearance of user input--I first thought they were leftover debugs when viewed in diff.

Do those changes seem reasonable to you?

Thanks for the thorough explanation. No need to share the code.

What `set_value` does is to:
1. Check if the new value is in the valid values (if any). Raise if not.
2. Do `self.value = value   <<<`
3. If there is a callback, call it, passing the new value.
4. If should_notify, send update to clients.

In other words, in the callback the value is already set to the characteristic, i.e. no need to do `set_value` there. I think this solves your problem, right?

If you want different behaviour of Characteristic, you can create a call the loader with a different type to instantiate as a Characteristic. @lk-geimfari I've already mentioned this in #267, but if one is defaulting the argument to `None`, which is [automatically pushed](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/fe4ea9cf1ee04f5a60e4ed928d8624b95b031e18/Lib/typing.py#L366) to `type(None)` or `NoneType`, then we should use `typing.Optional[the_type]`. In the example above, we'd have
```python
def randints(self, amount: Optional[int] = None, a: int = 1, b: int = 100) -> list:
        """Generate list of random integers.

        :param amount: Amount of elements.
        :param a: Minimum value of range.
        :param b: Maximum value of range.
        :return: List of random integers.
        """

        if amount is None:
            amount = 3

        return [self.randint(a, b) for _ in range(amount)]
```

When it comes time to distribute your code, it's important to identify dependencies that will need to be installed on new machines (which includes non-standard Python packages). For a large codebase with many import statements, it can be cumbersome to manually check which packages are non-standard, especially if it's been a while since you first started using some of them. For me personally, I needed to start running my project on a HPC cluster, so I had to send the sys admin a list of dependencies to install. Based on there being several similar questions on StackOverflow, I think it's safe to say that this is not an uncommon need.

@ribeaud So, here's how I'd propose to convert this to a helpful feature in the next release or two, tell me what you think:

**Implementation:**

Add a 'form' element to the ContentHandler class, so that you can supply, read from file, and template out form data as a request body.  This is internally URL encoded and concatenated with separators per the HTTP spec, as well as setting the default header (unless you choose to override this).


**Test syntax:**

```yaml
- test:
  - name: "I submit form data'
  - method: POST
  - body: {form: {'parameter': 'value', 'cheese': 'gouda', 'foo': 'bar'}}
```

And it would do all the above implicitly under the hood in the configure_curl method (or a helper method more likely, to keep that short).

With this you'd get template/variable binding integration, the ability to read from file, current encoding handling goodies, etc.  The catch is that it adds one more dimension to the combinatorial matrix that is the ContentHandler.

**Timeline:**

Currently I'm in the midst of major revisions to support Python 3, handle encoding more safely, and offer better support for dynamic content passing.  These would all drive potentially significant changes to the same code, so this is going to come after that (unless someone in the community has time to put together a PR).  

Since there's a way to do it currently (albeit clumsily) and side project time is limited at the moment, probably we're looking at the release *after* next unless I get a burst of time or a PR comes in.

Ok, I'm gonna make an executive decision, and declare that the PyPI tarballs are the official ones, and ignore the github-generated ones (at least for now.. I'm happy to revisit this later).

* the main install instructions (`pip install magic-wormhole`) depends upon the PyPI-hosted tarballs, and doesn't know about the github ones
* so we can't turn off PyPI's tarballs, and they must be sdists (and include PKG-INFO), not from git-archive
* there are no published install instructions that start with the github tarballs

It's a bummer to not have the easy traceability step from git SHA1 to git-archive tarball (and instead having a one-to-many SHA1->tarball conversion function, with a single distinguished/signed application of this function being used to make the official tarballs). But PyPI needs metadata that isn't commited to git.

So yeah, please update the watch file to point at PyPI, and verify the signatures that sit next to the tarballs there. For extra credit, we could automate something to compare the tarball's contents (but not timestamps or EGG-INFO or the missing extra files) against the github tarball, and compare the github tarball against a checkout of the github SHA1.

Maybe some future changes to Python packaging will make this step better :).

If the HMAC that protects the token's integrity depends on the user's password, we must fetch the user from the database before we can validate the token. This leaks existence of user ids through a timing attack -- which might not be a very large concern if you're using this relatively insecure library, but I'm still uncomfortable with it.

I prefer to stick with the current design which includes two levels of hashing:

- a HMAC managed by Django, which depends on `SECRET_KEY`, and ensures it isn't possible to forge a token; if a forged token is submitted, it fails the HMAC check, and that's it; nothing is leaked
- a hash of the user's password, in order to invalidate tokens on password change.

Without digging into the code, just from memory, I think I like the idea of adding a check on Credentials.expiration in the _boto3_connection_kwargs() method.

So... here's a thought: Maybe, what is now ``Connectable.credentials`` should really be ``AwsLimitChecker.credentials``. If it were an instance variable on the AwsLimitChecker object itself... I guess we'd need to pull it out of the Connectable classes (i.e. the first time we construct a Connectable class, we'd need to do something like ``self.credentials = someConnectableInstance.get_credentials()``), and then pass it in to subsequent Connectable subclass constructors, but I think it would net the same result as now (allowing all Connectable instances to share creds, especially when those are assumed role or MFA creds that we want to use as long as possible). We'd still need to do something on the boto3 side though... I guess instead of directly calling ``boto3.client()`` and ``boto3.resource()`` which [use boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/000f4e0b82bcf073b82deb5eb0e77b9f7427e8bd/boto3/__init__.py#L77), we'd need to explicitly construct a new [boto3 Session](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/session.html) per Connectable instance and store it in the instance, and call ``.client()`` and ``.resource()`` on that instead.

Even though this project is up to almost [10k downloads/month](http://jantman-personal-public.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com/pypi-stats/awslimitchecker/index.html), I'm still the only consistent contributor. I certainly don't have all the answers or best ideas, and it's really unfortunate for me to be working on something like this (it's my most visible/popular personal project by orders of magnitude) with no other humans to bounce ideas off of and sanity check me. @aebie and @cstewart87 if you think my suggestion is either crazy, unnecessary or unfortunately complicated, tell me so, and I guess we'll give the dict idea a try and see if anyone has issues with it. I'd really appreciate and be happy for the level of opinion and honesty you'd give someone on your own team.

Thanks!

This is clearly wrong and you can't use order by like that. Joinedload uses aliases where as the argument passed to order by does not. 

It should be something like this:
```python
current_translation = db.aliased(model.current_translation)
fallback_translation = db.aliased(model.fallback_translation)

query = (
    model.query
    .join(current_translation)
    .join(fallback_translation)
    .options(
        db.contains_eager(model.current_translation, alias=current_translation)
    )
    .options(
        db.contains_eager(model.fallback_translation, alias=fallback_translation)
    )
    .order_by(current_translation.name)
)
```

Note:
Query like this will be quite slow with many entries because even if models translation's name is indexed column PostgreSQL can't use this index when fetching the models.

> intersection and union should be instance methods and not class methods:

True. Note that the `set` class supports both the usage as a class method and as an instance method. For instance, the following works:
```python
set1 = {3, 18}
set2 = {3, 27, 42}
set1.intersection(set2) == set.intersection(set1, set2)
```
I am not sure on the best way to do this. Something like this seems to work:
```python
class A:
    def foo(self, *args):
        if not isinstance(self, type): # this is called as an instance method
            args = (self,) + args
        print(args)
```

-----

> Some methods aren't implemented:

Also true. From [Python's documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/stdtypes.html#set), the (hopefully exhaustive) list of methods that are currently missing in pyroaring is:
- `isdisjoint`
-  `issubset`
- `issuperset`
- `difference`
- `symmetric_difference`
- `copy`
- `difference_update`
- `symmetric_difference_update`
- `clear`

-----

> Changing these behaviors would be a breaking change and it's understandable if you don't want to break existing code.

It would not be a breaking change if we manage to make the methods like `intersection` work both as instance and class methods.

AFAIK, on Windows it is required to [install a special driver](https://github.com/libusb/libusb/wiki/Windows#driver-installation) instead of the default one for libusb to do anything with the device. This also means that until you uninstall that driver, the device will only be usable through libusb.
I used Zadig a bit (years ago), and IIRC it makes it easy to (un)install this driver, so it shouldn't be more than a slight annoyance for developing - but may be a more serious issue if you want to distribute your program (one extra step, needing admin rights, and if not undone user's android device will seem unusable from the computer).

Ah. In the docs it says:

> Limitations: django-object-actions expects functions to be methods of the model admin. While Django gives you a lot more options for their admin actions.

So I assumed this wasn't the case.

In terms of justifying the usefulness of some of these - I use (1) passing in a url to provide navigation between closely related models. For instance a Purchase Order might have an object tool that says "Edit Account Details". UX wise I felt these belonged with other actions.

I actually define row actions via a dict for a couple of reasons:

1. Urls need a way to specify a 'label'. For consistency I allow this for all action types
2. I wanted a way to disable an action but still have it appear. I felt it was poor UX for buttons to be sometimes there and sometimes not. It was better to have it there but disabled so that the user becomes familiar with the potential choice of actions.
3. Related to this a dict allows me to also supply a tooltip. This allows me to explain why an action is disabled. As a bonus I get to provide general help text for actions that aren't disabled.

How useful is any of this to your project? It's fairly simple to implement. My code is already fairly similar in layout (I basically used your app as a template for row actions)

Okay, i'm finally beginning to get this figured out.

So I've been doing some reading up on SIP ([RFC 3261](http://www.rfcreader.com/#rfc3261) and [RFC 6665](http://www.rfcreader.com/#rfc6665) are the big ones used).

I also managed to capture a full session using wireshark while i was testing with the Blink SIP client.

A few things i've found out:

SIP is just an "initiation" protocol, all it does is setup another protocol which actually streams the data. that protocol seems to be RTP (which from a preliminary look, should be easy to convert to something like an MP4 or something else which we can easily use output from). The SIP messaging responds with information on how to access this RTP stream, and it seems like it's randomized somewhat so we can't just skip the whole SIP messaging step and grab the stream.

It also looks like the version of SIP here uses some extensions (the second RFC I linked) to "subscribe" to something. That subscription request is being denied which is why i'm guessing these streams always end like 30 seconds after I start them.

I'm fairly sure we can get the SUBSCRIBE stuff to work by just impersonating the ring client, which means we can get a full stream (including 2-way audio if you want it) working.

All that being said, I'm gonna give this a shot. I haven't worked with python for like 7 years, so i'm hoping if I can get a "proof-of-concept" working, someone else can take the reins and actually get it working well.


My next step is to get some of this SIP messaging working in python. I'm going to use the Twisted library which does seem to have some support for SIP ([here's the protocol definition in twisted](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/twisted-17.1.0/src/twisted/protocols/sip.py)).

On a closer inspection we currently don't support GTG as we only check if plugins succeed from checks. A failure means the service is in non-operational state. We don't have a "partially working" check. We only offer "working" or "not working".

I guess we could add a `text/plain` response with `"OK"` easily and people could register the health check endpoint under `/service/asg` (or `/service/gtg`) URLs if they so want. 

The question is whether we want to offer `text/plain` as the default reply if no `Accept` header is defined - at the moment we render `text/html` and changing that behaviour could break some functionality.

The correct full implementation, I guess, would be to offer

- `^/service/asg/?$` with the current health check and `text/plain` as the default response type
- `^/service/gtg/?$` URL with partially working checks - i.e. add "downgraded" option for statuses

What do you mean by registration ?
An user object is automatically created if you use an external token but I can't really handle registration as some people will not have the same fields, actions and behaviors for their user.

I can help you by posting some examples but I will not implement it in this package unless wanted by a lot of people.

```
# You can use this as a simple serializer
class UserSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    is_active = serializers.CharField(read_only=True)
    date_joined = serializers.DateTimeField(read_only=True)

    class Meta:
        model = User
        fields = ('email', 'password', 'username', 'first_name', 'last_name', 'is_active', 'date_joined',)
        extra_kwargs = {'password': {'write_only': True}}

# And this viewset
class UserAPIView(GenericViewSet, RetrieveModelMixin, UpdateModelMixin, CreateModelMixin):
    """
    ### Interact with users

    - **Methods**:
        - list: *Post*
        - detail: *Get*, *Put*, *Patch*
        - me: *Get*
            - returns the current user
    - **Permissions**:
        - authenticated user required on detail
    - **Returns**:
        - the user
    """
    queryset = User.objects.all()
    serializer_class = UserSerializer
    permission_classes = [permissions.IsAuthenticated]

    def get_object(self):
        obj = super(UserAPIView, self).get_object()
        if obj != self.request.user:
            raise Http404
        return obj

    @list_route()
    def me(self, request):
        user = get_object_or_404(self.queryset, pk=request.user.id)
        serializer = self.serializer_class(user)
        return Response(serializer.data)

# Add these to your urls
    url(r'^api/', include('rest_framework_social_oauth2.urls')),
    url(r'^api/users/?$', UserAPIView.as_view({'post': 'create'}), name='user-list'),
    url(r'^api/users/me/?$', UserAPIView.as_view({'get': 'me'}), name='user-me'),
    url(r'^api/users/(?P<pk>\d+)$', UserAPIView.as_view({'get': 'retrieve', 'put': 'update', 'patch': 'partial_update'}), name='user-detail'),
```

It is likely to be related to OpenBLAS.
- Testing with package built from source cannot reproduce the issue, which is openblas `0.2.20`.
- The latest pip package updated openblas to `0.3.1` and it reproduces the issue. 
- Package built mxnet from source with OpenBLAS `0.3.1` and `0.3.0` also reproduces the issue.
- All above versions don't reproduce the issue on GPU context.

OpenBLAS 0.3.1 seems to have a similar non-deterministic bug reported at: 
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/27960
https://github.com/xianyi/OpenBLAS/issues/1666

The issue can be reproduced with simple network with only convolution and dense layers:

```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import nd, gluon
from mxnet.gluon import nn

image = nd.ones((1,3,224,224))
# net = gluon.model_zoo.vision.get_model(model_name, pretrained=True)

net = nn.Sequential()
net.add(
    nn.Conv2D(channels=5, kernel_size=1, activation='relu'),
    nn.Dense(65),
    nn.Dense(1)
)

net.initialize()

val = net(image)[0][0].asscalar()

print(mx.__path__)
for i in range(10000):
    tmp = net(image)[0][0].asscalar()
    if not tmp == val:
        print("Error!! : val=%f, tmp=%f, i=%d" % (val, tmp, i))
        break
    print("All Good!! : val=%f, tmp=%f, i=%d" % (val, tmp, i))
```

```
No (valid) module for script 'test_script' found
```

## Reason:
- sometimes, this error was  caused by `your codes have bugs`.
- like: 
    - your code `import a third party package`, and `the package has bugs`, 
    - It is difficult for you to find errors `caused by  the third party package`.


## How to fix it:
- so, you need use `try ... catch` to get the `exception`. 
- then after you fix the bug, you will find the `runscript` is ok now.


```python
# runscript file:
def run():
    try:
        from xxx import  xxx    # maybe the third party package has bugs.
        do_xxx()
    except Exception as e:
        print(e)
        raise e
    
```

Watchman recursively watches files that are contained within a filesystem tree.
Symlinks can point to an arbitrary location on the filesystem.
Changes to symlink targets that are outside the tree are not observable.

Why not simply resolve the symlink and watch its target?  Because it isn't simple.  Here are a handful of reasons that make this a difficult prospect.  This is not an exhaustive list, it's just a few reasons I can rattle off the top of my head:

* The target of the symlink may not exist at the time we want to establish the watch, and neither may any of the parent directories in the symlink path.  The operating system will not allow us to watch a path that does not exist
* Any of the directory components in the target symlink path may themselves be a symlink and may change at an arbitrary time in their lifecycle in a way that is not detectable unless we watch every single directory component in the symlink target path
* The target of the symlink, or any of the directory components of the symlink target, may be a remote filesystem that doesn't support change notifications
* Each additional watched directory consumes more filesystem watch resources. There are finite limits on these, and not every system allows raising these limits.
* Because the hypothetical watches that we'd establish for each symlink are distinct from the watch used for the root of the real tree, we lose certain ordering guarantees when processing change notifications from the kernel.  We wouldn't be able to tell whether a change to the symlink target happened logically before or after changes that we observe in the main tree.  This important property is used to ensure that filesystem changes have been observed up to the logical point in time of a query.  More details on this can be found at https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cookies.html
* Filesystem changes are fraught with TOCTOU (time of check, time of use) issues already.  Adding user-space resolution of symlinks increases the chances of more TOCTOU bugs dramatically; any component of the symlink path may change while we're processing information about them.

Correctly handling all of the above would be tremendously complex and still be error prone.  As a result, it is unlikely that we'll ever add support for resolving and tracking symlink targets.

I'd recommend that you explicitly export that from your Makefile.

You may also want to try out https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/watchman-make.html; that runs commands in the foreground with your environment set.  You'll need to do a HEAD install if you're using homebrew:

```
brew install --HEAD watchman
```

and it will install `watchman-make` in `/usr/local/bin` and/or in `/usr/local/Cellar/watchman/HEAD/bin`

```
watchman-make -p '**/*.ml' -t all
```

I'm personally not a fan of accepting types other than strings, since many types can be stringified but this won't result in correct behavior (dicts, lists, objects, etc).  I'd suggest dropping that part and keeping the API strict, something like this:
```
if not isinstance(string, six.string_types):
    raise ValueError("Non-string passed to quote_with_encoding")
if not isinstance(string, six.binary_type):
    string = string.encode('utf-8')
```

Looking at that code in `_utils.py`, I think we always end up specifying a user-agent header in the call to `self._session.request(headers=headers)`, so this overrides any custom header values you've set on the `apiclient` object.  The code in `_utils.py` should instead:

* Look for a "user-agent" header in the `headers` parameter passed to the request.
* Look for a "user-agent" header in the `headers` property of `self._session`
* Only set the default header if it wasn't present in either of those places.

Are there any precedents in this space? I’m trying to draw parallels with things like IntelliJ checkstyle inspections etc.

The config sources are:

* files in a user’s home directory (user configs)
* files in the project (project configs)
* within the file (e.g. # noqa)
* IDE settings (the ones in question)

I’m going to take linting as the example here. Obviously “within the file” should win always, so let’s dismiss those settings.

My thinking is that project configs are configured to be verified at CI/build time. They basically say, “I don’t care who works on the project, they need to conform to this style guide”.

User configs (in their home directory) are there for when project configs don’t exist. You’re working on a project that doesn’t have any style preference explicitly checked in, so you’re going to apply your own preferences for your work only.

So project configs override user configs when running the linter.

IDE settings then exist to customise the user’s editing experience. In applying these settings they are choosing to ignore what the project says, and go with their own style. So they should override the project settings.

* If you want to default project configurations, you should do so in your user configs.
* if you want those configs verified at build time, they should be checked in to the project
* if you want to ignore the project settings and apply your own, then you configure your editor.

At least that was my train of thought implementing this. I could probably argue for the other way as well though! So keen to hear your thoughts.

Actually, scratch that, I think you get *worse* error messages using `readable()` than you would just using `hasattr(fileobj, 'read')`.

```
        for download_manager_cls in download_manager_resolver_chain:
            if download_manager_cls.is_compatible(fileobj):
                return download_manager_cls
        raise RuntimeError(
            'Output %s of type: %s is not supported.' % (
                fileobj, type(fileobj)))
```

So if the file isn't opened correctly you'd get a vague `Output <open .... 'w'> of type file is not supported`.  Still enough info to figure out what went wrong, but not as explicit as a "file not opened for writing" when we try to write to the file.

```python
def get_obj(content, vimtype, name):
    """
    Return an object by name, if name is None the
    first found object is returned
    """
    obj = None
    container = content.viewManager.CreateContainerView(
        content.rootFolder, vimtype, True)
    for c in container.view:
        if name:
            if c.name == name:
                obj = c
                break
        else:
            obj = c
```

I did not mean to imply that this in itself is a security problem, sorry for the confusion. Uncaught exceptions naturally leads to aborted authentication by itself. I was just arguing for having clear and straightforward error handling in u2flib to avoid mistakes when using the library.

I agree, both U2FError, ValueError and any Python exception must be handled and lead to authentication failure. However, they must be handled in different ways since they are different:

- When the user provides garbage or bad credentials I probably want to log the request and return a HTTP 400-ish error code. Currently a `ValueError` from the `json` library may happen in this case.

- u2flib may have bugs that we do not yet know about that also happens to throw `ValueError`. In that case, replying status code 400 to the client is not correct. We also want to log that and notify the developers and then maybe report the bug here. :)

The only way to use u2flib currently is basically to wrap all calls with `except Exception:` to be sure to handle every case. That hides bugs and is a python anti-pattern.

This is roughly how I would like to use u2flib:

```py
try:
    try:
        start_authenticate(...)
    except U2FError:
        return HttpResponse(status=400, 'u2f authentication failed')
except Exception:  # This part is usually handled by Django, Flask etc
    logger.exception('u2f authentication crashed')
```

requests has a similar problem and solution (instead of u2f authentication they send http requests) that is solved with a base `RequestException`: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/_modules/requests/exceptions/

Hi @dstufft, thanks for your work on virtualenv, happy to see an rewrite. Some thoughts for food: 

- Is click useful? I think a core tool like virtualenv must be standalone. No Dependency conflicts please!
- Selected options (`--pip`, etc) must be shown when installing a environment (like "options: xxx, yyy").
- With `--system-site-packages`,  `--no-setuptools` and `--no-pip` must be the default (non sense to install pip and setuptools into the global Python, I use virtualenv to keep it clean).
- `--version` option. Always practical.
- `--requirements` options to be passed on to pip, and an `--index`option, to load from another server or an local directory.
- The ability to use a cache directory to share packages.
- A default directory for installation (from a configuration file or an environment variable).
- A command to change the environment's Python interpreter. Useful when you upgrading your global Python.

I'm a huge fan of virtualenv but I dislike the activate's way. It would to cool to have a shim to use a default environment and to switch easily between environments. An example:

```
$ which pip
~/.virtualenv/defaultenv/bin/pip
$ virtualenv shell test
$ which pip
~/.virtualenv/test/bin/pip
$ virtualenv create myproject
$ virtualenv shell myproject
$ which pip
~/.virtualenv/myproject/bin/pip
```

Another command would be `use` to call  a environment "one-shot":

```
$ which pip
~/.virtualenv/defaultenv/bin/pip
$ virtualenv use test pip install django
Use ~/.virtualenv/test/bin/pip
...
$ which pip
~/.virtualenv/defaultenv/bin/pip
```

It's like that I manage day-to-day multiple versions of Python (with Pyenv).

This looks good, I think it should work exactly as intended.

The step `Get workflows and review states` is actually executed twice by intention; it just snapshots the current workflow and state of all objects, once _before_ activating the new workflow, once _after_ activating.
This is important because when activating the new workflow the current workflow state information may get lost (depending on the state names of the old and new workflows).
Having a "before" and a "after" state snapshot allows us then to update the review state according to the defined mapping.

Summarized, this happens:
- Snapshot of workflows and review states
- Activate new workflow
- Snapshots of workflows and review states
- Fix review state according to mapping and both snapshots

I would indent the logging message though, since the workflow is actually activated within the context manager. This will reorder the log so that it makes more sense:

All existing tests should be reviewed and changed to the given-when-then approach. Here is a good [example](https://github.com/scrapinghub/dateparser/pull/26/files#diff-36128d81467a740315c9e5f49860c291R182) how it should look in the end:
```python

    def test_dates_parsing(self, date_string, expected):
        self.given_utcnow(datetime(2012, 11, 13))  # Tuesday
        self.given_local_tz_offset(0)
        self.given_parser()
        self.given_date_string(date_string)
        self.when_date_is_parsed()
        self.then_date_was_parsed_by_date_parser()
        self.then_period_is('day')
```

Hi @user500, we've introduced `TIMEZONE` setting to dateparser. You can use it as follows. By default, all dates are considered in `UTC`. If timezone setting is specified, dates are converted to the given timezone. 

```
# relative date
In [10]: dateparser.parse('2 hours ago')  # UTC
Out[10]: datetime.datetime(2016, 1, 20, 7, 54, 40, 195695)

In [9]: dateparser.parse('2 hours ago', settings={'TIMEZONE': 'US/Eastern'})
Out[9]: datetime.datetime(2016, 1, 20, 2, 54, 26, 450596)
```

```
# full date
In [17]: dateparser.parse('21 december 2015, 22:10') # assumes UTC
Out[17]: datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 21, 22, 10)

In [18]: dateparser.parse('21 december 2015, 22:10', settings={'TIMEZONE': 'Asia/Karachi'})
Out[18]: datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 22, 3, 10)
```
Benefit is that you don't need additional logic to match the options rule by rule.

For those finding this via Google later, I did have a workaround which is to implement zookeeper watches to pass the reset requests down to the consumers that do own the partitions. The details:

I set up a directory in zookeeper with a structure like

    /reset-watches/my-consumer-group/my-topic/1
    /reset-watches/my-consumer-group/my-topic/2
    ...

Where names of the nodes (`1` and `2` above) are a Kafka partition number and the value of that ZK node is what offset I want them to reset to (or empty if there are no pending requests). Then each consumer sets up watches for every node that corresponds to the Kafka partitions that they own and sets the rebalance callback to update its watch list. When the watches trigger they read the new offset from the value of the node and because they own the partitions they are able to reset them normally using `consumer.reset_offsets` and empty out the watched node.

Implementing this safely requires getting the ZK transaction semantics right to deal with race conditions between all of the events that can occur (new reset requests being created, rebalances, completed reset requests, updating the ZK node to reflect that it's done) and may need to be changed for different tradeoffs of how often these rewinds need to happen, tolerance for reprocessing the same message, etc.

Ok, I think I've looped through this in my head until it exploded now :)

There's one more thing that the old `Producer._update_leaders` did that hasn't made it over: because it held locks on all `OwnedPartitions`, it would block new `enqueue` calls while an update was going. That is important for message order, I think. With the new `_update`, you could potentially call `produce` in the middle of it running, and the newly submitted message would queue-jump past all the messages that were temporarily in `_waiting_messages`.

The conda recipe I put together in #105 will automatically install Leiningen, and I think that is probably the more appropriate way to solve this issue for now.  This also won't be a factor if/when we get rid of the Clojure part (as per #136).  In light of both of these facts, I'm closing this for now.

There's a topology shutdown period that's equal to your tuple timeout value where it waits for everything in-process to finish.  It stops reading new tuples from the spout, so there's no new work coming in, but it still gives you an opportunity to finish anything in-flight.  If you're waiting for more tuples to come in before checking the time and releasing the next batch, those tuples never show up and you never handle tuples which are waiting -- the machine just locks up.

This doesn't sound *too* bad if you have good bookkeping of what's been acked, but if you're using Kafka with auto-ack, those tuples have now fallen into a black hole.

I'm not sure renaming the partitionId to sequenceId is the best course of action. So far the use of sequenceId has been as a piece of debugging information that is purely informational and has no effect on the algorithm. partitionId, on the other hand, when provided, does affect the output of the KNN classifier by excluding subsets of the training vectors. 

I could see removing the partitionId functionality if it isn't needed, and implement sequenceId instead at the region level. Or both sequenceId and partitionId can coexist since they have entirely different functionality. But causing sequenceId to have side effects would be a departure from current usage in the rest of the codebase.

It's possible to [change the reponse code used in validation](https://github.com/mozilla-services/cornice/blob/master/cornice/errors.py#L15), yes.

However, I don't think there is an easy option to set this up with cornice when the validation is done by the Colander schema directly.

The creation of the `Error` object is done [on each new request](https://github.com/mozilla-services/cornice/blob/11d7a3b28f0463871670dd3a96cecf18b00611f3/cornice/pyramidhook.py#L141-L142). One simple way to do what you want would be this (in your app initialisation):

```python
from pyramid.events import NewRequest
from cornice.errors import Errors

def attach_custom_request(event):
    setattr(request, 'errors', Errors(request, status=422))

config.add_subscriber(attach_custom_request, NewRequest)
```

I think I could make it so `schema_type()` is no longer a static method, but then it's getting rather hackish.

> Do you see any advantage on this? Currently the dict gets updated so if we have the same key, e.g. email both from acount_info and from form.data the one from the form will be taken.

Mainly for security. As it is now it's safe, but it's very easy to introduce unwanted security issues. E.g someone adds a poorly validated form field that overwrites a trusted account info value allowing the end-user to pass in any value. Or, an oauth app sets a value in account_info, and uses it later in account setup, however in-between account info data gets overwritten by form data (since you run ``account_info.update()``).

I would rather be careful in this case, since we're dealing with user authentication and sign up. Hence, being explicit about origin of values makes more sense to me.

I think the issue that Travis is having ("module zc has no package ...") is due to the fact that `coverage` is installed in the virtualenv, along with `zc.buildout`, creating a `zc` namespace package that's available and visible to the buildout. When the virtualenv coverage runs the buildout script, that `zc` is winding up on sys.path ahead of what the buildout sets up, leading to these errors. It seems like I've run into this before, but I'm not finding it (I thought I remembered leaving a comment in a .travis.yml somewhere). I can imagine two solutions:

1. Don't use buildout in .travis.yml. Just 'pip install -e .[test]' and go from there.
2. Make buildout.cfg handle installing coverage. And then run `bin/coverage run bin/test` so that everything is in the buildout environment.

I have not yet tested this yet, but the theory is if you have [`frida-server`](https://www.frida.re/docs/ios/#with-jailbreak) running on your jailbroken device, then you can connect objection to any running app with `objection -g <name> explore` where the name can be found using `frida-ps -U`. Not specifying a gadget name with `-g` simply defaults objection to `Gadget`, which is the name used for the gadget in patched iOS applications.

You can have as many apps installed as you your developer license allows for. I think the free one will let you install two/three apps. Patched applications by default will have the gadget name of `Gadget`, so objection will simply look for that and connect to it. Backgrounding an app will cause objection to lose connection, so if you start a second application, running `reconnect` will connect objection to that application.

Hope that helps.

It sounds like you're trying to use python-for-android as a general purpose Python interpreter for Android - it isn't.

python-for-android is an application packaging framework to package whole Python apps as Android apks. It is not designed for embedding inside another Android app, and currently the implementation has a hard requirement that you use [Kivy](http://kivy.org) to write your program.

You could certainly look through the python-for-android code, and use it to help you build your own Python interpreter and have your existing app load it, but that's beyond the scope of p4a right now. If you like Python, though, you should look at Kivy and see if it would work for you.

This is why you should profile.  The FFT is taking 0.9% of the time here.  So fftw tweaks aren't going to help at all.  Nor are the array copies significant.

The biggest thing is the k-space interpolation of the interpolated image.  We use a Quintic interpolation in k-space for interpolated images, which [Bernstein & Gruen, 2014](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2014PASP..126..287B) found was necessary to avoid significant errors in the shapes of the drawn images.  (I'm guessing Giuseppe doesn't do that, so that's probably the biggest time difference between the two.)  I don't think you probably want to mess with that if you're using this code for shear estimation.

However, we could speed things up a lot by only doing this step once for the PSF, rather than every time, since it's not changing between model draws.  There's no easy way to do this in the current version of GalSim calls, but you could roll your own custom drawFFT function that would do a single setup step to make the initial k-space image, draw the PSF and pixel components onto it and save that.  Then for each model, drawK() onto another image with the same size, bounds and multiply by the PSF/pixel image and finish the wrap/rfft calls.  Since your galaxy is analytic, those steps should be extremely fast.

Ying and I reproduced the results of the Sholl analysis of the axon of the cell OG061123A1-4-IDA.ASC in Check_Sholl_analysis.zip done by @lidakanari end of last year and tried to find the reason for the differences between NL (Neurolucida) and NM (NeuroM).

The visualisation of the Sholl analysis results given by Neurolucida (NL) could not help because the figures NL gives for the Sholl analysis are very imprecise. 
- First, they are 2d projections, although what is computed is the number of intersections with spheres.
- Second, the resolution of the neurites is low. The neurites are much more tortuous than in the Sholl analysis figures (see last Figure, also from Neurolucida).
- Third, Neurolucida does not give explicitly the coordinates of the spheres center (the so-called reference point). Even if the coordinates are computed the same way as in NM, there can be small numerical differences, which, in combination with the tortuosity of the neurites may lead to the observed differences between the two applications.

My conclusion is that both applications (NL, NM) may be correct and consistent in themselves. The observed differences may result from differences in the computation of the reference point (the spheres center) in addition with the tortuosity of the neurite. This cannot be verified though, since the reference point coordinates in NL are not reported.

I include a small report with the details and the figures. The Sholl analysis figures start with radius 260 um and 1200 um respectively and proceed in 20 um steps, exactly as the analysis.


[ShollAnalysis.zip](https://github.com/BlueBrain/NeuroM/files/1041472/ShollAnalysis.zip)

Thinking some more about desired behavior when starting up as master times out, I think wait_for_failover is unnecessary and counter productive.

When we time out starting up, immediate stop is desired to allow for quick failover. However, we don't need to wait for failover. We can just initiate start as a standby. If someone else promotes all is well, but if no one does we will have a head start recovering from the crash and will eventually promote ourselves.

Current rewind logic does have a problem with this as need_rewind gets cleared if we start up as a leaderless replica. Going to push a patch for that, but I haven't yet convinced myself that it's completely safe.

What about trying to fetch cluster state from the DCS before shut down? That would mean that we would observe relatively recent state when making decisions both in the HA loop or in shutdown.

There is still a race in that the HA loop might be in the middle of running when pause is set. So something this ordering is still possible, even if unlikely:

* Ha loop fetches cluster state
* Set cluster to pause
* Stop Postgresql
* Ha loop discovers that Postgresql is not running and starts it, because it still thinks pause is on.

One idea would be to have `touch_member()` set a timestamp, a `pause --wait` mode would then check the member list for last check-in timestamps, set pause mode and then poll for members to either update their timestamp or go away. This might have some gotchas related to DCS consistency models.

If there is default value, then it will be automatically filled in the swagger UI, but still does not work for RESTAPI call

For eample, I have an API http://localhost/v1/getResult?num=0
where num is optional and 0 is default and means no number limit.
If I call http://localhost/v1/getResult
There will be an internal 500 error
The other problem is, 0 will be shown on Swagger UI, which is confusing without explanation.
Sometimes it is better not to have a default value for optional parameters.
A better solution would be adding "=None" for the optional parameter if there is no default value in the defualtcontoller.py

So for the above example, I would prefer "def get_result(num=None)" in the controller.
If there is a default value, then also "def get_result(num=0)"  for example.
In both case, you can actually treat num optional, and call http://localhost/v1/getResult

Chalice does not have any explicit integrations with swagger from an API definition perspective. The point of Chalice is to define your API in the python code itself so using swagger for this would run counter to one of the main selling points of Chalice. 

The only place Chalice currently uses swagger is as an implementation detail in how it deploys the API Gateway interface. Once the API Gateway has been deployed you can always fetch the API definition in swagger format using the [Export](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-export-api.html) API Gateway API. 


If you are just asking for a feature to get documentation into API Gateway itself then there is a PR up right now for sourcing the API documentation from the docstrings of the chalice route functions. You can go give a thumbs up if you are interested in this feature: https://github.com/aws/chalice/pull/681

This bad state only happens when `mutual_authentication` is `REQUIRED` or `OPTIONAL`. My theory for why this happens:

1. It appears that server authentication can only occur after client authentication.
1. Server authentication occurs in `handle_other()`.
1. Giving up and going to `handle_other()` after hitting 2 consecutive 401s results in an invalid Kerberos operation if server authentication is attempted. `_negotiate_value()` is probably returning `None`.

I'm fairly certain this is a bug. The lease maintenance thread is trying to make sure a deadline gets **extended** for any messages that the subscriber is still responsible for, but in doing this **extension** it actually **reduces** the lease time for your message (this is a mistake in your and my opinion).

There is no equivalent high-throughput / highly concurrent implementation you can downgrade to. [Here][1] is every Pub / Sub release. Version `0.28.0` was when the rewrite happened, so downgrading to `0.27.0` or earlier you won't be subject to this bug, but the library was totally different at that point. (We don't have any published docs for that version of the library, but I'd be happy to work with you to show you how to build the docs locally.)

[1]: https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/pubsub/releases.html

A working example built off yours:
```console
mkdir reproducible
cd reproducible
virtualenv env
. env/bin/activate
pip install pex
mkdir greatpackage

cat << EOF > setup.py
from setuptools import setup, find_packages

setup(
    name="greatpackage",
    version="0.1",
    author="bqm",
    packages=find_packages(),
    scripts=["greatpackage/script.py"]
)

EOF

cat << EOF > MANIFEST.in
include *.py

EOF

touch greatpackage/__init__.py

cat << EOF > greatpackage/script.py
if __name__ == "__main__":
  print("Hello world!")

EOF

pex --no-wheel -s . -e greatpackage.script -o output.pex
./output.pex
```

See this discussion for reference: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!searchin/pants-devel/Trying$20to$20make$20a$20single$20executable$20file$20with$20PEX/pants-devel/WDMzxuW0ybs/hmblP6i_vbcJ

It's clunky, but I managed to solve this issue this way:

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: "2010-09-09"
Transform: "AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31"
Resources:
  SomeFunction:
    Type: "AWS::Serverless::Function"
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-someFunc"
      Handler: "index.handler"
      CodeUri: "."
      Runtime: "nodejs6.10"
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 5
      Environment:
        Variables:
          ANOTHER_FUNC_VERSION: !Sub "${AnotherFunctionV2.Version}"
      Policies:
      - AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
  AnotherFunction:
    Type: "AWS::Serverless::Function"
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-anotherFunc"
      Handler: "magic.handler"
      CodeUri: "."
      Runtime: "nodejs6.10"
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 5
      Policies:
      - AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
  AnotherFunctionV2:
    Type: "AWS::Lambda::Version"
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !Ref AnotherFunction
```

So in this scenario, `SomeFunction` might have some code that invokes `AnotherFunction` using a specific version. Any time I update the `AnotherFunc` code, I rename the `AWS::Lambda::Version` resource to the next version up, e.g. `AnotherFunctionV3`, then package and deploy.

The serverless approach is nice and clean but not much mature in the api keys/stages/usage plans area. It creates an extra "Stage" (named "Stage") even if we define our own stage, and the field is mandatory.

I couldn't find a permanent solution for the dirty stacks, and usage plans inside the cloudformation script, so I wrote a bash script that deleted the stage named "Stage" and creates api keys, usage plans then assigns keys to plans. Parsing JSON in the bash, clearing the stages, takes away the clean and neat solution of serverless in the Api gateway : (

Here is the script:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

echo "==============================="
echo "Get id of the api (set output param ApiId in CF)"
echo "==============================="
APIID=$(aws cloudformation describe-stacks \
--stack-name my-stack \
--query 'Stacks[0].Outputs[?OutputKey==`ApiId`].OutputValue' \
--output text)

echo "==============================="
echo "Delete the Stage env"
echo "API ID: ${APIID}"
echo "==============================="
aws apigateway delete-stage \
--rest-api-id ${APIID} \
--stage-name 'Stage'

echo "==============================="
echo "Create usage plan for stage name: v1"
echo "API ID: ${APIID}"
echo "==============================="
USAGEPLANJSON=$(aws apigateway create-usage-plan \
--name "my-usage-plan" \
--description "Usage plan for low usage of api" \
--api-stages apiId="${APIID}",stage='v1' \
--throttle burstLimit=2,rateLimit=1)
echo ${USAGEPLANJSON}

echo "==============================="
echo "Created usage plan for v1"
echo "API ID: ${APIID}"
echo "Getting Usage plan Id for:"
echo "==============================="
USAGEPLANID=$(echo ${USAGEPLANJSON} | python -c 'import sys, json
print(json.load(sys.stdin)["id"])')

echo "==============================="
echo "Creating api key for v1"
echo "==============================="
APIKEYJSON=`aws apigateway create-api-key \
--name 'my-api-key' \
--description 'Used for security of the admin api' \
--enabled`
echo ${APIKEYJSON}

echo "==============================="
echo "Created api key for v1"
echo "==============================="
APIKEYID=$(echo ${APIKEYJSON} | python -c 'import sys, json
print(json.load(sys.stdin)["id"])')

echo "==============================="
echo "Create usage plan key for v1"
echo "API ID: ${APIID}"
echo "USAGE PLAN ID: ${USAGEPLANID}"
echo "API KEY ID: ${APIKEYID}"
echo "==============================="
aws apigateway create-usage-plan-key \
--usage-plan-id ${USAGEPLANID} \
--key-type "API_KEY" \
--key-id ${APIKEYID}
```

You would be using class `DataFrameMapper` as usual. The fact that the fitted pipeline will undergo conversion to PMML does not give it any superpowers.

> I am trying to make label encoding of the target values part of the pipeline.

Target value transformations is not a standard Scikit-Learn practice. It is assumed that the `y` parameter (of the `Classifier.fit(X, y)` method) is in its "original" representation, and the classifier class will transform it internally (if necessary).

So, in case of multi-class classification, you would be passing a Pandas dataseries as the `y` parameter, and the classifier class will construct a `LabelEncoder` and apply it behind the scenes.

> Keeping the LabelEncoding outside makes it a success:

Your pipeline should be fitted just fine even without explicit `ytl = LabelEncoder().fit_transform(yt)`.

Please not that `DataFrameMapper` is only applied to the `X` parameter (of the `Classifier.fit(X, y)` method). Other column mappings (eg. `ytl` in the first two code examples) will be ignored.

1. Instead of the very advanced identification of Python 2 classifiers in setup.py, I think you should hard-code the names of the packages to move. It will be way easier to configure and understand.
   
   This approach will eliminate the string code + the need to try-fail importing things in Python 3. Thanks for the remark. If GSE60361_C1-3005-Expression.csv was directly downloaded from GEO and you only changed the extension of the file then the problem is twofold:

> I'm not clear on what the need is for separate scp_to vs scp_from commands?

The interface we had before didn't make sense to me:
```
unit = model.units['ubuntu/0']
unit.scp('localpath', 'ubuntu/0:remotepath')
```

Having a `Unit.scp` method where you have to specify the name of the unit seems silly. Here are the alternatives I came up with:

1. Split it up into `scp_to` and `scp_from`:
```
unit.scp_to('localpath', 'remotepath')
unit.scp_from('remotepath', 'localpath')
```
2. Use a simpler prefix like `remote`:
```
unit.scp('localpath', 'remote:remotepath')
```
3. Move the `scp` method to `Model`:
```
model.scp('localpath', 'ubuntu/0:remotepath')
```

I'm not a fan of APIs that require string manipulation -- seems error prone, and harder to read when you have to build strings dynamically -- so I went for option 1. I'm open to changing it however.

Seems six.raise_from behaves differently on Python 2 to Python 3 :-(
six.raise_from(exc_value, exc_value_from)¶
Raise an exception from a context. On Python 3, this is equivalent to raise exc_value from exc_value_from. On Python 2, which does not support exception chaining, it is equivalent to raise exc_value.

Maybe could use this one:
six.reraise(exc_type, exc_value, exc_traceback=None)
Reraise an exception, possibly with a different traceback. In the simple case, reraise(*sys.exc_info()) with an active exception (in an except block) reraises the current exception with the last traceback. A different traceback can be specified with the exc_traceback parameter. Note that since the exception reraising is done within the reraise() function, Python will attach the call frame of reraise() to whatever traceback is raised.

We can't do this because the spec makes no guarantees about public.glyphOrder, it's just an ordering you should use when set. In TruFont we use public.glyphOrder as a superset of all glyphs present in the font. So we put all glyphs in the font in there, and those which don't have outlines defined are treated as template glyphs (i.e. ones that you will fill up at some point since they're part of your glyphOrder), that's how we store these. And if public.glyphOrder is a subset of all the glyphs when opening the font, it gets filled up to make sure it represents all glyphs (it's used for various things e.g. looping of glyphs). So while what you suggest can be done, the subsetting will be overwritten or treated differently if you open up in an editor and save.
