> Also, when you say QueryStats is broken, what exactly do you mean?

I should have said `ConnectionStateStats`, apologies.

In your [current master](https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/blob/943af9e7b8f2ee6a02af487ba5e2dc6101482579/src/collectors/postgres/postgres.py#L387-L419) you have the following query:

```sql
SELECT tmp.mstate AS state,COALESCE(count,0) FROM
       (VALUES ('active'),
               ('waiting'),
               ('idle'),
               ('idletransaction'),
               ('unknown')
       ) AS tmp(mstate)
LEFT JOIN
     (SELECT CASE WHEN waiting THEN 'waiting'
                  WHEN state = 'idle' THEN 'idle'
                  WHEN state LIKE 'idle in transaction%'
                      THEN 'idletransaction'
                  WHEN state = 'disabled'
                      THEN 'unknown'
                  WHEN query = '<insufficient privilege>'
                      THEN 'unknown'
                  ELSE 'active' END AS mstate,
             count(*) AS count
       FROM pg_stat_activity
       WHERE pid != pg_backend_pid()
       GROUP BY CASE WHEN waiting THEN 'waiting'
                     WHEN state = 'idle' THEN 'idle'
                     WHEN state LIKE 'idle in transaction%'
                         THEN 'idletransaction'
                     WHEN state = 'disabled'
                         THEN 'unknown'
                     WHEN query = '<insufficient privilege>'
                         THEN 'unknown'
                     ELSE 'active'
                END
     ) AS tmp2
ON tmp.mstate=tmp2.mstate ORDER BY 1
```

If you run this on 9.3 you'll get this:

```
      state      | coalesce 
-----------------+----------
 active          |        0
 idle            |        1
 idletransaction |        0
 unknown         |        0
 waiting         |        0
(5 rows)
```

But if you execute the same query on 9.6 you'll get this:

```
ERROR:  column "waiting" does not exist
LINE 9:      (SELECT CASE WHEN waiting THEN 'waiting'
                               ^
```

That's because in Postgres 9.6 [`pg_stat_activity`](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.6/static/monitoring-stats.html) the `waiting` column got removed.

The JMESPath specification says, about the value returned for a search:

> The return value for an identifier is the value associated with the identifier. If the identifier does not exist in the JSON document, than a null value is returned.

So if the value associated with the identifier is `null`, the specification deliberately conflates that with the “does not exist” case. Hence this library is behaving correctly by the specification.

I maintain the specification is broken by design. The “does not exist” behaviour should, in order to be easily detected, be entirely disjoint from any valid value that could be returned by a match. Raising an exception would be an appropriate implementation.

On 16-Oct-2017, Jeff Cliff wrote:
> ok so that was 3 things

Thank you for working to address this.

The questions I was raising here are not so much about how to change
the files. Rather, they are requests to the copyright holders, to
clarify explicitly what is the grant of license.

> 2) "GPL is not a grant of license" has me really confused as to what
> is being asked for, exactly.

The question is for the copyright holders to answer: What set of
license conditions do you, the copyright holders, explicitly grant to
recipients of this work?

A grant of license is some explicit text, from the copyright holders
of the work, that says something like “you, the recipient of this
work, are free to do x, y, and z, under [some specific set of
conditions]”.

The specific set of conditions can't be guessed reliably. It might be
“the GNU GPL version 2 or later”, or might be “the GNU GPL version 3
only”, or might be “the GNU GPL version 3 or later or the Apache
License version 2 or later”; or, well, any weird set of conditions, in
my experience.

This is one reason why the GNU GPL has useful instructions “How to
Apply These Terms to Your New Programs”. But again, the “you” there
can only be the copyright holder; we can't make those statements on
their behalf.

Without that, we aren't in a position to guess the intent of the
copyright holders.

```bash
"${CONDA_PREFIX}/etc/conda/activate.d/"
```

instead of

Even so, it's pretty easy to achieve the same thing with `conda install package; conda remove package`.

Yes, they are separate actions.  At some point, we actually need to combine the actions at an individual package level (not for the full fetch/extract operation), and then make individual transactions out of them.  We also can then parallelize them.

I think the best approach here for this immediate PR is to change the return value of `make_actions_for_dist` from a two-value tuple to a three-value tuple, where the third value is a new PathAction.  And then, I think the only place where the new action is actually used is in that last return value, where we're actually downloading packages

```
        # if we got here, we couldn't find a matching package in the caches
        #   we'll have to download one; fetch and extract
        cache_axn = CacheUrlAction(
            url=record.get('url') or dist.to_url(),
            target_pkgs_dir=first_writable_cache.pkgs_dir,
            target_package_basename=dist.to_filename(),
            md5sum=md5,
        )
        extract_axn = ExtractPackageAction(
            source_full_path=cache_axn.target_full_path,
            target_pkgs_dir=first_writable_cache.pkgs_dir,
            target_extracted_dirname=dist.dist_name,
        )
        return cache_axn, extract_axn, new_axn
```

The `--ignore_empty_list` option for `curator_cli` doesn't prevent the error message.  It hasn't been fully implemented yet (in `curator_cli`), because its whole reason for being was to allow Curator to proceed to the next action if it was only an empty list condition that caused the error.  

With `curator_cli`, there is no next action, as they are singletons.  You are free to script another action after this without regard to the output of an error.

When I eventually implement this fully, it will still not necessarily prevent the error message. It will simply guarantee that it exits with a zero exit code.

```
Command
    az storage entity insert: Insert a new entity into the table.
        Inserts a new entity into the table. When inserting an entity into a table, you must specify
        values for the PartitionKey and RowKey system properties. Together, these properties form
        the primary key and must be unique within the table. Both the PartitionKey and RowKey values
        may be up to 64 KB in size. If you are using an integer value as a key, you should convert
        the integer to a fixed-width string, because they are canonically sorted. For example, you
        should convert the value 1 to 0000001 to ensure proper sorting.

Arguments
    --entity -e     [Required]: A space-separated list of key=value pairs. Must contain a
                                PartitionKey and a RowKey.
    --table-name -t [Required]: The name of the table to insert the entity into.
    --account-key
    --account-name
    --connection-string
    --if-exists               : Specify what should happen if an entity already exists for the
                                specified PartitionKey and RowKey.  Allowed values: fail, merge,
                                replace.  Default: fail.

```

```
                                later than the expiry time. visibility_timeout should be set to a
```

Welcome back @ingalls! I'm out of my expertise when it comes to address localization, glad to have all the comments from people who actually put this data to use.

My main complaint is the code we currently have is not good enough to apply to all our data. I cringe at the idea that we might be formatting "Nord Mauerstraße" in Berlin as "North Mauer Street". (Made up example.) I feel strongly we shouldn't degrade data. So my first thought was just to stop trying to do any expansions.

But there's so much interest in doing good expansions I'm newly encouraged! We can steer OpenAddresses towards doing expansions where we know we can do a good job and then improve our expansion capability. I imagine there has to be prior art from national post offices, etc we can use.

Specific proposal:
1. Create a new GitHub project for street name expansion. Seed it with the Javascript code [in csv.js](https://github.com/openaddresses/openaddresses-conform/blob/572b7a8066db107f028ecd5407fda0b2c7041d84/Tools/csv.js#L268) and the Python code [in expand.py](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/ditch-node/openaddr/expand.py). Make them separately installable libraries with NPM/PyPi. Share a suite of test data so we ensure the two libraries behave consistently.
2. Remove the expansion code itself from openaddresses-conform and openaddresses-machine, include our libraries instead.
3. Add localization to the expansion library. My first thought was to do it by region, but @ingalls proposal of a conform option is also appealing.

I think the near-term outcome is to  make the conversion code expand the output only for the US locale, where our current code works well. Then we can start expanding the code to other locales incrementally.

The object pairs hook is just a callback which receives a list of pairs.  You could probably implement an efficient function which returns a normal OrderedDict :)

Something like:

```python
def pairs_first(pairs, first_pairs):
    before = [pair for pair in pairs if pair[0] in first_pairs]
    after = sorted([pair for pair in pairs if pair[0] not in first_pairs])
    return collections.OrderedDict(before + after)
```

and then `functools.partial` `first_pairs` and pass that as the `object_pairs_hook`

Something like:

```python
json.loads(s, object_pairs_hook=functools.partial(pairs_first, first_pairs=...))
```

I override all `_handler` to add their own processing middlewares.
Also the logical division of functions will not be superfluous.

Skip middleware example:
```python
async def wrap_middlewares(self, match_info):
    handler = match_info.handler
    skip_middlewares = getattr(handler, '__skip_middlewares__', set())

    for app in match_info.apps:
        for m, new_style in app._middlewares:
            full_path = '{}.{}'.format(m.__module__, m.__name__)
            if '__all__' in skip_middlewares:
                break
            elif full_path in skip_middlewares:
                continue

            if new_style:
                handler = partial(m, handler=handler)
            else:
                handler = await m(app, handler)

    return handler
```

Ok, I understand what's happening here:
 - My HTTP server is a basic aiohttp server with only a **GET** handler
 - a HEAD handler is added automatically by aiohttp
 - This HEAD handler correctly strips the body from the response returned by the GET handler
 - **But** it does not strip the `Content-Length` header. (if my GET handler return a `web.Response(text='okuie')`, I get a `Content-Length: 5` header in the response to a HEAD request, with no body
 - And all this keeps the connector from re-using the connection, which create new SSL connection for every requests and looks a lot like a memory leak (even though it's definitely not a leak)

@Lukasa I get the perverse part about making an open source tool that becomes popular - the more we demand of you the more your "gift" becomes a burden. 

The thing is, configuring a CA bundle to a sensible system default is not a code-level problem as you suggest. Python doesn't seem to come with a sensible OS level CA bundle lookup path, which is presumably why you made this package and why so many others import it.

However, writing a wrapper is not feasible for me - the code in question happens to be running running in 3rd-party immutable Docker images. I'm generally only able to set run-time environment variables when the images are run as containers.

I don't expect you to be entirely sympathetic but the snarky emoji isn't warranted either. I'm not the first to struggle with the design choice of "doing one thing and doing it well" (your mantra here and that's fine). It's just that there's no escape hatch either - in my case, it's doing one wrong thing, and doing it well. Heck, I'm so downstream from this package that it shouldn't even matter, but this is where the root of my challenges with getting these images to pickup internal CA bundles is rooted.

> I'm surprised to hear `ext` seems cryptic; I thought it was pretty standard for "extension", "extended", etc. 

It's growing on me a little bit, but I still prefer more explicit names.

> In #333, I envisioned that the extras would be exposed as attributes on the container object: `somefield.extra.description`. (This is similar to the context object present during import/export.) What are your thoughts on this?

I'm concerned that this introduces all of the issues regarding name clashes and dictionary methods that we've talked about in #375.  It seems acceptable and even intuitive to me that a container that holds *custom, external* data is left completely as-is, but I also see the appeal of attr-based access.

Consider this.  Stylistically a lot of people will probably prefer to declare their extra data like this:

```python
class Person(Model):
    first_name = StringType(
        required=True,
        extra=dict(
            title='First Name',
            description="The person's first name")
        )
    age = IntType(
        extra=dict(
            description="The person's age",
            as_sql_col='NUMBER')
        )
```

So, I think a simple solution is for schematics to provide an attr-dict util class that users can use if they like:

```python
class Person(Model):
    first_name = StringType(
        required=True,
        extra=AttrDict(
            title='First Name',
            description="The person's first name")
        )
    age = IntType(
        extra=AttrDict(
            description="The person's age",
            as_sql_col='NUMBER')
        )
```

I've also been thinking about your concerns about the term `metadata`.  I could be wrong, but I don't think that most developers feel strongly that metadata must be purely descriptive.  What we're talking about is data related to other data (the field), and I don't think that someone who wants to associate a sql column with a `schematics` field will be confused or discouraged when they spot "metadata" in the code or docs.  Also, not that it's a strong argument, but "metadata" is the term that [`marshmallow`](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) uses for the same purpose.

My 2 c.  I'll take a poll of the devs here at work to see what they think.

I've been thinking about use cases for dynamic choices, and I would expect a pretty common scenario to be one where the set of valid choices depends on another field on the model. But that case cannot be addressed with `choices`; you have to use a model-level validator anyway:

```python
def validate_foo(cls, data, value, context):
    ... # Compute choices.
    if value not in choices:
        raise ValidationError(...)
```

That's already pretty succinct. Using a callable as `choices` may be slightly more convenient but gives you a less powerful version (without access to `data`). If the set of choices is large, you likely don't want to replicate it in the error message, which means you'll have to add `messages` to accompany `choices` or make a custom type. At that point, one must ask if a validator method might in fact be the cleaner approach.

So basically my concern is that perhaps `choices` isn't the right tool for the job if the job is complicated. Callables would only accommodate a rather specific case where the list of choices cannot be computed in advance but the thing it depends on is external to the model.

Please don't take this as steadfast opposition. It's just that there have been PRs that were accepted with too little review IMO, and I'd rather vet everything thoroughly in the first place. I'm on the fence on this because personally I don't see it as very useful and it adds a bit of processing overhead for every field. I'm hoping one of the other owners would cast a vote one way or the other.

The confusing separation of aggregate errors into `ModelValidationError` and `ModelConversionError` is gone. The distinction now only exists at the field level where it makes sense, while aggregate errors are always delivered as a `DataError` instance. The individual `ConversionError` and `ValidationError` instances are preserved in the `DataError` message structure.

Jasper, do you want the full Jacobian of a vector-to-vector function or do you just want its diagonal? If it's the Jacobian itself that you want, then you have to loop over the gradients of each output component with respect to the input vector, as Matt shows. If you want just the diagonal, and if the off-diagonal elements are zero (the conversations we've had makes me think that's what you're talking about) then you can just use the gradient of the sum of the output, and the calculation happens in a single pass. For example:

```python
>>> import autograd.numpy as np
>>> from autograd import grad
>>> def jac_diag(fun):
...     return grad(lambda x : np.sum(fun(x)))
... 
>>> x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 5)
>>> jac_diag(np.sin)(x)
array([-0.9899925,  0.0707372,  1.       ,  0.0707372, -0.9899925])
>>> np.cos(x)
array([-0.9899925,  0.0707372,  1.       ,  0.0707372, -0.9899925])
```

I could add the wrapper functions `jacobian` (Matt's `D`) and `jac_diag` if you think they'd be useful...

What I am looking for is attributes. In particular, the basic notion in abms is mostly that the background (space) is featureless. The agents have rules / policies / attributes. The kind of modeling that I am doing is expanding that notion a bit. The context can have attributes / features. One of those features is memory, i.e., what has happened in that space. Potentially, this memory may have a time constant over which it degrades. 

I have implemented a framework for doing this. Here is what I have so far:
- Dimensions - a multi dimensional space along the lines of space. At init, the number of dimensions is specified. The way I keep track of cells allows me to add attributes  to the cells. I have not fully implemented this or tested this yet.
- dynspace (dynamic space) used for movement calculations in n dimensions. It is a subclass of dimensions. 
- realspace - that keeps coordinates as real numbers but is a subclass of dynspace. 
I have done some real basic testing. And the framework seems to be working so far. I have not done any performance analysis, cleanup, testing framework,  etc. yet.

Right now I am trying to get the graphics to work a bit more easily.

At some point, when the code is a bit more polished, I could ship it to you to review  to see if you think it would be helpful to add to mesa.

So I think we can first implement some relative simple changes to the Strategy interface which allows varying prices based on the selected options. In a later phase we can extend this based on experiences learned.

The idea is to pass a dict with the selected options to the `pricing_policy` and `parent_pricing_policy` methods. This requires us to also add the options to the `fetch_for_product` method.

The dict can be as simple as:

```python
options = [
    {
        'code': 'my_option',
        'value': 'my option value',
    },
]
```

@bmw imagemagick's `convert` command gives flags a similar [ordering based semantics](http://www.imagemagick.org/Usage/basics/#why).

The other minimal syntax that I think is available is to use something like `--webroot-map` in a way that actually implies the domain names.  I [started implementing it](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/tree/webroot-map-and-flags) on top of #1594 but it's going to take a while to fix the tests, and probably JSON is the wrong way to format the dictionaries.

That could ultimately look like:

`--webroot-map {example.com,www.example.com:/var/www/example, m.eg.is,eg.is,www.eg.is:/var/www/eg}`

That's a bit of a nuisance to parse because it can't be split on `,`.  The dict entries could be separated by `;`, but then the `;` needs to be escaped.  Or the strings could be protected by `"`, as occurs in `JSON.parse`, but that's no longer really minimal.

The letsencrypt client expects the CSR in DER format which is not the default for OpenSSL.  Use "-outform der" when creating the CSR.

I think the client should also accept PEM format as this is the default used by tools like OpenSSL and is also the output format the client uses for the certificates.

> Putting renew() in main.py won't really simplify cyclic deps since cli.py will still import from main. Given that the two imports are in exactly the same place, we don't seem to loose much elegance from the second one :)

Putting `renew()` in `main.py` breaks `cli.py`'s dependence on `renew.py` and `renew.py`'s dependence on `main.py`. While this simple change does not eliminate our cyclic dependency problem, it reduces it to `cli.py <-> main.py`. Instead of having 3 cyclic dependency problems, we have 1.

> I also thought about splitting renew() while writing the patch, and concluded there wasn't a nice, quick way to do it.

Assuming you don't want to spend much more time on this, what about simply taking `renew()` as is and putting it in `main.py`? I think having all subcommands in one place as well as removing the majority of our cyclic dependencies is worth this simple change.

> Perhaps parts of storage.py could be refactored into renew.py too; I don't have an opinion on that yet.

Maybe, but I don't think that should happen in this PR.

I think the names `rm` and `erm` are a little subtle.  We don't want to make it too easy for people to accidentally delete all their files.  I'd like to suggest an alternative command-line syntax:

```
b2 rm [--report] [--versions] [--glob] [--regex] <bucketName> <thing_to_delete> ...
```

Without either option, it would delete only exact matches, and won't delete all files.  With either `--glob` or `--regex`, it would do the kind of matching specified.  You would have to go to a little effort to delete all files.

We need the hook to recompile code upon fields changing (either from the constructor or from field types changing across invocations), we need the hooks to attempt the serialization/deserialization first before falling back to the methods in `marshmallow.marshalling`, and we need the hook for `Nested` fields to use the same JIT as their parent.

I would rather not dictate how to store descriptions in marshmallow core. Descriptions are metadata that don't affect behavior, and there's already a way to store and access metadata:

```python
field = fields.Field(description='A fine field.')
field.metadata['description']  # 'A fine field.'
```

This flexibility allows client libraries to use whatever name is appropriate (e.g. "description", "help", "desc", "info").

OK, with a little more investigation it seems that during `dump`, `included_data` is expected by fields to be a dict keys by an (id,type) tuple.

During `load`, `included_data` takes the value from the input `data['included']`, which will be a list if it's a compliant JSON API document; which is good because `_extract_from_included` is expecting it to be a list.

The main problem arises when you use the same `Schema` instance to do a `load` of a payload with included data, then a `dump`. The `included_data` attribute is not reset, leading to `Schema` expecting to find a dict, but getting a list instead.

I think that `marshmallow_jsonapi.schema.Schema` should have a pre_dump method which resets the `included_data` back to an empty dict.

At the moment, there is no difference, but I'd like to implement deserialization too - otherwise receiving metadata will not work.

is there any information you can share (or pointer to the SFrame/GL github, for the flow that was used in the _internal.search functionality, which I could replicate externally to the modules, for example leveraging some of the text_analytics components to create my own functions that leverage the functionality in turicreate to obtain similar results?

Now, let me share a bit about how i'm using it.

I have been using the SFrame module to analyze, cluster and group a couple of public data sets. Two in particular.
1) all retirement plans in the US since 2009. The SFrame modules have been extremely useful. The data is openly available from https://www.dol.gov/agencies/ebsa/about-ebsa/our-activities/public-disclosure/foia/form-5500-datasets
2) all 990 IRS tax filings for Exempt Organizations https://docs.opendata.aws/irs-990/readme.html

Both of these datasets are based on human entered data, which can both vary from year to year, and in some cases are intentionally differentiated. Particular search fields are company names. Various attempts to normalize the company names led to more complexity and edge cases. Eventually I loaded the data into elasticsearch which allowed me to search the data, but not so useful for pythonic analysis. Inverted index searching was the way to go.

Eventually I stumbled upon the _internal.search model function and started to use it for pythonic data analysis - taking a name, looking for similar ones using the search criteria, and adding those to a new column in the SFrame, so i could then use that to normalize the data (using EIN's as the key). Amongst other uses, such as creating a dashboard that lets a user search for a company/plan name across multiple fields (Company name and Plan name in the retirement dataset can reinforce and rank higher something that otherwise would not) Additionally there are nested objects inside of another column that has service provider data, and i do an index on that information to search for plans that for example have "fidelity" as a service provider to the retirement plan. It does a great job providing me a with a quick queryable list of candidates, without loading all the data into solr or es to generate the queries, and keeps everything inside of python, i can also generate models for subsets, and deploy the model to AWS lambda (my intention) to support much quicker, analysis for multiple iterations that are needed on some functions. (i could also use ES for this going forward, which is my current approach in lieu of the search model).

One hypothetical example is "BLUE CROSS BLUE SHIELD" while they are all different entities (regional and local), they all belong to a collective group. This is noted either in their plan name, mailing address, administrative name, etc.) and in different formats, spellings, abbreviations, etc. These are across both 401k and 403b plans. 

I create multiple search models (k) is for 401k plans and (b) is for 403b plans

```
m_srch_sfmain_k = gl._internal.search.create(sfmain_k, features=['SPONSOR_DFE_NAME', 'PLAN_NAME', 'SPONS_DFE_CARE_OF_NAME','SPONS_DFE_DBA_NAME'], verbose=False)
m_srch_sfmain_k.save('m_srch_sfmain_k')
m_srch_sfmain_b = gl._internal.search.create(sfmain_b, features=['SPONSOR_DFE_NAME', 'PLAN_NAME', 'SPONS_DFE_CARE_OF_NAME','SPONS_DFE_DBA_NAME'], verbose=False)
m_srch_sfmain_b.save('m_srch_sfmain_b')
```

LightFM is designed to be able to deal with this problem.

You do this via user features: as long as each user is described by a set of features and these features are shared between the training and the test set, you will be able to make predictions in the test set.

Suppose you describe each user by their unique id and their country of origin. While training, you estimate parameters for the id feature and the country feature. At test time, you can use the country feature to provide prediction even for a user whose id isn't part of the testing set. (You couldn't do this if you didn't have the country feature, because there would be no way of estimating the representation for a new user.)

This is exactly equivalent to the [StackExchange](https://github.com/lyst/lightfm/blob/master/examples/stackexchange/hybrid_crossvalidated.ipynb) example, but instead of using item features (in this case, question tags) you would use user features (by passing them to the [fit](http://lyst.github.io/lightfm/docs/lightfm.html#lightfm.LightFM.fit) and [predict](http://lyst.github.io/lightfm/docs/lightfm.html#lightfm.LightFM.predict) methods).

> Replaces the file with a copy

> all file system changes will be flushed to disk.

these are implementation details and shouldn't be in the API. atomicity is the key property here. implementation strategy and durability should not be specified.

> def replace(self, filename=None)

IMHO this is the wrong API. no file system in real use that i know of is fully transactional. this API provides and abstraction that is not well supported underneath. in the end, it will create a copy of the file in all supported OSes; this is why i think it is the wrong API.

a more useful API would be saveAs(self, file=None) that saves pending changes to a copy of the file somewhere else. API help would include a note that, if 'file' parameter is missing, it will save to a temp file and then replace the original as atomically as possible. (and stating that this saving mode is safer and slower than the usual save is OK; but it should be noted that the usual save is safe enough for normal use.)

> This operation can be significantly slower than save().

yes, the saveAs() API could be added. but the important issue here is that it doesn't fix the problem with the current implementation of save()! furthermore, you leave it to the app programmer to decide what to do, and they know little or nothing about risks. what do you expect them to do? wouldn't all of them either choose replace()/saveAs() or ask you a question personally?

plus the 'safe save' is only needed when the ID3v2 tag is added or resized, because loosing the tag is not as bad a loosing the payload, and the tag is so small that a partial save is really unlikely. and with replace/saveAs you force a copy on each save, when 99% of the time it is not needed.

what is really needed is a safe 'payload scroll' only when resizing the ID3v2 tag. IMO, the normal save should be upgraded to behave in a **safe enough** manner, and that is it. this entails copying the file but only when a payload move is needed. more free space is needed by the new save? too bad. i rather have a reasonably safe save that complains the disk is full than a dangerous save. if the disk is full, users will have to deal with that very soon, so let them deal with it now.

so to recap, my proposal is:

1. fix save(): its current implementation is unsafe and unfit for all uses. most existing client programs will never upgrade to replace() anyway, so replace won't fix the problem. most newer clients will choose replace() and your library will become slow and hated.

2. optionally (and at a later time) add saveAs() API. include with it a proper implementation of "save to temp and rename" when no target file is given.

Since you can reason about recursive functions by generating Horn clauses in Z3, we do already offer ways to deal with theorems about recursive functions. It does require the front ends to be aware of such encodings, but this amply described in several papers and is essentially just a way to symbolically write down Hoare logic assertions with placeholders (my home page contains some introductory material and papers by Andrey Rybalchenko). Just asserting recursive equations invokes engines that are currently not tuned specifically for checking satisfiability in the context of recursive definitions.

Hi guys,

I've stumbled upon the exact same issue in one of my company's projects. We're using python3 and I'm 100% sure there's no **u'** missing on any model's __ str__ method. Yet, the UnicodeDecodeError exception still occurs when trying to import a csv file containing utf-8 encoded characters in the django admin.

It turns out that adding the **encoding** param with value of **utf-8** to the TempFolderStorage.open's method seems to solve the problem, at least in our environment.

Here's a quick'n'dirty fix in case anyone googles this issue trying to find a solution, like I did a few hours ago.

First we subclass the TempFolderStorage class, adding said param:
```python
import tempfile
from import_export.tmp_storages import TempFolderStorage

class Utf8TempFolderStorage(TempFolderStorage):

    def open(self, mode='r'):
        if self.name:
            return open(self.get_full_path(), mode, encoding='utf-8')
        else:
            tmp_file = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False)
            self.name = tmp_file.name
            return tmp_file
```
Then we point the new class as IMPORT_EXPORT_TMP_STORAGE_CLASS in settings.py:
```python
 IMPORT_EXPORT_TMP_STORAGE_CLASS = 'path.to.storages.Utf8TempFolderStorage'
```

I've never personally been a fan of pytest and rarely have used it, and in researching this issue I'm beginning to remember why.

Because of the (completely non-pythonic) introspection magic that pytest uses, I don't think you're going to be able to use fudge the same way you can unittest or nose. The decorators that fudge provides are convenience wrappers to do much of the `clear_calls` and `verify` steps that you mention above. Since pytest isn't compatible with these standard decorators, you're going to need to do these steps manually. Another option would be to build a plugin for pytest (as seems to be the standard for the pytest community) which provides these conveniences or provides a pytest compatible wrapping.

If there is an existing pytest-fudge plugin I'm not finding or one is created I'd be happy to add it's mention to the documentation. But I feel that since pytest is the library that is doing something out of the ordinary any other fix is outside of the scope of fudge.

```
class Tag(models.Model):

class Article(models.Model):
    headline = models.CharField(blank=True, null=True, max_length=128)
    tags = models.ManyToManyField(Tag, blank=True, through='ArticleTagThrough')


class ArticleTagThrough(models.Model):
    article = models.ForeignKey(Article, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    tag = models.ForeignKey(Tag, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    primary = models.BooleanField(default=False)
```

I don't want users to set more than one tag as "primary" per article, so in the admin, I use an inline with an InlineFormSet validation:

```
class TagFormSet(BaseInlineFormSet):
    def clean(self):
        super().clean()
        taggings = [form.cleaned_data for form in self.forms if form.cleaned_data]
        primary_tags = [tagging['tag'] for tagging in taggings if tagging['primary']]
        if len(primary_tags) > 1:
            raise forms.ValidationError('Articles must not have more than one primary tag.')


class TagInline(admin.TabularInline):
    model = Article.tags.through
    formset = TagFormSet
    verbose_name = 'Tag'
    verbose_name_plural = 'Tags'


class ArticleAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    model = Article
    inlines = [TagInline]
```

I found subtle issue about reset detection for testcode/vectoradd.v.

```verilog
  always @(posedge CLK or negedge RST_X) begin
    if(!RST_X) begin
      cnt0 <= 0;// RST_X is not reset for MEM_WE
    end else begin
      if(state == ST_INIT) begin
        MEM_WE <= 0;
    end
  end
```
This case, in current Pyverilog, RST_X is regarded as reset signal of MEM_WE.
But in my PR version, RST_X is not regarded as reset, so raise Exception in _createAlwaysinfo.
1) Completely same as current Pyverilog.
2) Raise Exception as my closed PR.
3) Not regard as reset, but not raise Exception.

1. Depends on what you mean by synchronization. Temporal sync (making sure all cameras capture frames at the same time) is problematic since each camera has its distinct clock. For spacial sync, you could match objects from one camera in frames of another through image registration or estimate fixed relative camera models. Both techniques are not trivial, and are beyond the scope of librealsense right now. 
2. Using the same USB3 controller for more then one realsense device is not recommended. Bandwidth of USB3 is limited and you will inevitably loose frames. If you're using separate USB controllers, you will still need a powerful PC or you will have frame drops at the kernel driver level. 
3. The interference depends on camera type. In short, you can capture depth from two R200 devices pointed at the same target, but not from two SR300/F200. You can read more about that under [Camera Specs](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/camera_specs.md)

Perhaps @ddiakopoulos will be able provide additional information.

I don't know exactly why is complaining, but I can see one thing you need to change.

In your build method you are invoking the dlib CMakelists.txt file, that right, but this CMakeLists doesn't know anything about your declared dependencies (the lib paths, flags etc). 

You can paste these lines before the `self.run("mkdir build")`:

```
conan_magic_lines = '''project(dlib)
include(../../conanbuildinfo.cmake)
CONAN_BASIC_SETUP()
'''
replace_in_file("dlib/dlib/CMakeLists.txt", "project(dlib)", conan_magic_lines)

```

But unhappily it couldn't be enough to let dlib cmake script to locate all your conan dependencies, it depends A LOT in every case. Make a package for a library with dependencies could be hard if you don't link with CONAN_LIBS directly.
Maybe the find_package just works and it locate all your conan dependencies, but maybe you will need to replace more things in the CMakeLists.txt like the `target_link_libraries` to link against $CONAN_LIBS etc. I recommend you to ensure that dlib CMakeLists.txt is finding correctly all your deps in the conan directories using MESSAGE for example.

opentracing_instrumentation module uses its own thread-local storage for propagating the current span. Flask instrumentation doesn't know about it, you would have to register the span manually, e.g. via `with RequestContextManager(span=span):` (see README).

Once OpenTracing 2.0 for Python is released, there's going to be a standard propagation mechanism, and  both flask_opentracing and opentracing_instrumentation will be upgraded to use that.

@tleonhardt I'll be happy to run with this one, while I've never used NixOS, I have done lots of packaging work on other linux distros.

My initial, but far from comprehensive review of the test output leads me to believe there are several problems:

1. Many of the tests fail because they can't locate certain files (i.e. `tests/scripts/empty.txt`, `tests/scripts/recursive.py`, etc). It appears that the entire `tests/scripts` directory is missing. Those files are included in the source distribution, not created by the test suite. I don't know how the NixOS build system retrieves and unpacks source files, but either it grabbed the wrong stuff from git, or it didn't get unpacked properly. If my assertion that the root cause here is a packaging configuration problem, it should be relatively easy to resolve.

2. There appears to be quite a few failures with something similar to this:
```
>           assert err.startswith(expected_start)
E           AssertionError: assert False
E            +  where False = <built-in method startswith of str object at 0x10a6ac0>('.\n----------------------------------------------------------------------\nRan 1 test in')
E            +    where <built-in method startswith of str object at 0x10a6ac0> = 'E\n======================================================================\nERROR: runTest (cmd2.Cmd.run_transcript_te...n\n----------------------------------------------------------------------\nRan 1 test in 0.000s\n\nFAILED (errors=1)\n'.startswith
```
These tests are all parametrized instances of the same logic, and they all depend on files in the `tests/transcripts` directory. If that directory is missing like `tests/scripts` is missing, these tests would all fail too. I think it's likely that these two classes of failures share the same root cause.

3. We have made certain assumptions about the underlying Linux OS capabilities, which might not be true of NixOS, or probably more likely, might not be true of the NixOS build environment. For example, our test suite assumes that if you are on MacOS or Linux that you have an executable `vi` in your path. cmd2 will run fine without it, but the test suite assumes it's there. The NixOS build environment doesn't appear to have `vi` in the path, so the test fails. I'll have a fine-toothed look at these failures and create a definitive list of platform assumptions and dependencies, which should be relatively simple to add to the NixOS build configuration.

I've put inbound webhooks on hold until after the 1.0 release (see #4), because there seemed to be no interest in them. (So, thanks for expressing interest!)

If you're looking for something right away, I'd suggest just implementing your own Mailgun inbound webhook. It should be a pretty simple Django view. (You could borrow Anymail's [Mailgun webhook validation code](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/v0.6.1/anymail/webhooks/mailgun.py#L28-L39) if that would help.)

By the way, you can use Anymail for *outbound* email while providing your own *inbound* email webhook. The two actually aren't all that connected.

The long-term plan for Anymail inbound handling will be very similar to [sent-mail tracking](http://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/latest/sending/tracking/): there will be a new Django signal for inbound events, and a new `AnymailInboundEvent` structure to normalize common inbound email parameters between ESPs (making it easier to switch or use multiple ESPs at once).

That makes sense.  The [`EmailMultiAlternatives.message()`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/core/mail/message.py#L251-L276) generated message ID gets regenerated on every call to `.message()`; but `mail.outbox` stores the `EmailMultiAlternatives` instance itself and not the serialized output of `.message()`.  As a result, a test can't use the generated output of `.message()` to predict the message ID that you'd get on a future call to `.message()`.  That's why I provided the `Message-ID` header in the test case, to bypass that auto-generation.

Moving away from `.message()`'s implementation and doing something more deterministic could help avoid confusion in the test case, sure.  Basing it on `len(mail.outbox)` would work in unit test contexts, but wouldn't ideal for localhost development, at least as long as you expect mesage IDs to be unique.  Everytime you restart the dev server, `len(mail.outbox)` would be reset to 0, and then you'd be reusing message IDs again (and potentially running afoul of unique constraints).

Perhaps the implementation for generating a message ID really needs to be different between test and console/localhost development cases, where test message ID's are deterministic based on something like `len(mail.outbox)`, but console message IDs are based on clock time like the current implementation.  Would be easy to implement a `get_message_id` method on the test backend, and have the console backend which derives from it override that.

> does your suggestion of putting the list in the dict work, given that the dict is part of the subclass rather than the pybind11-managed class?

It should still work because `tp_traverse` and `tp_clear` are inherited (non-`dynamic_attr` classes could set those functions without setting `Py_TPFLAGS_HAVE_GC`). But you have a good point about Python code being able to delete the special key from `__dict__` -- so best disregard my suggestion.

Regarding the alternatives that you proposed: (1) AFAIK this wouldn't work for subclasses of non-GC types (they wouldn't have the pointer in their instances). (2 & 3) I'm not a fan of either opt-in or opt-out. As you mentioned, it would be difficult to decide which one to use. (4) I don't think removing weakref support is an option.

Another possibility is keeping an `std::unordered_map` of `instance*` -> `patients` (the map would go into `internals`). That may be trading a bit of memory overhead for a bit of runtime overhead (map lookup on object destruction) so I'm not sure it's necessarily better, but it's an option.

> What's the next step?

Can you add tests for the situations that you found? (Keep the existing fix.) That way we can be sure about the source of the issue and that any proposed fix does actually work (also in light of PyPy vs CPython differences). Then we'll see about minimizing overhead.

Yeah I looked into it a bit and James added the `exclude` parameter when he made the parser able to parse conditionals on Debian based systems. Once the two tasks you identified above are resolved, I think we should remove it entirely as I don't see why we'd ever want to use it if we're properly able to parse conditionals.

To build on this, notice how [some command line arguments](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/a7a8e060e37fad21a9702ac0f143fd39609c8c2d/certbot/cli.py#L846) use [flag_default()](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/a7a8e060e37fad21a9702ac0f143fd39609c8c2d/certbot/cli.py#L270) while [others do not](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/a7a8e060e37fad21a9702ac0f143fd39609c8c2d/certbot/cli.py#L850). This is inconsistent and limits how useful `certbot.constants.CLI_DEFAULTS` can be in testing. If all defaults were defined in this dictionary, solving #4434 could be as simple as making a class with this in `setUp()`:
```
self.config = mock.MagicMock(**constants.CLI_DEFAULTS)
```
and changing a couple values such as file paths to a temporary directory.

You should be able to control the names of the executables and the paths Certbot uses when searching for things like vhosts by setting values on the command line or in a [configuration file](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#configuration-file). > So I understand that I can get an unescaped path from augeas, but is there a way to pass back an unescaped path to augeas and have it not blow up when looking for that file.

You can perhaps rewrite queries to use an expression like: `aug.match('/files/etc/apache/sites-available/*[label()="a,b.conf"]')` but that's quite a bit of effort as you'd have to split the path up and construct a complex-looking query.

You can keep and use your existing `_escape` implementation at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/3182/files#diff-bdd584c1dc4f4d6316df485617bec545R1003, which I think is your best option.  Additional special characters are `][|/=()!` (`name_follow[]`, https://github.com/hercules-team/augeas/blob/master/src/pathx.c#L126-L133).

> or maybe one option would be to add aug_escape_name to the python implementation of augeas and wrap things leaving augeas with self.aug.get(get_file_path('/augeas/files%s/path' % path)) and aug_escape_name(vhost.filep) - does that sound right?

Yes, nearly - aug_escape_name will also escape `/` according to https://github.com/hercules-team/augeas/blob/master/src/augeas.h#L373-L388, so you'd split the path by `/` and then pass each component through aug_escape_name().

I'd just stick with your existing _escape implementation and use that when you want to re-escape a path to use in an Augeas lookup.

Let's Encrypt, the Certificate Authority (CA) Certbot uses by default, and similar CAs [don't allow creation of certificates for IP addresses](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certificate-for-public-ip-without-domain-name/6082/25). To get an SSL certificate through Let's Encrypt, you'll need a domain name. To get an SSL certificate for an IP address, you would have to use a different CA.

In case you weren't just using it as an example: 127.0.1.1 is a [loopback address](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5735#section-4), so the [baseline requirements](https://cabforum.org/wp-content/uploads/CAB-Forum-BR-1.3.0.pdf) which apply to all CAs would prohibit issuing a certificate for that address.

The change `argparse`'s behavior I described above happened in 2.7.4 which came out in April of 2013. Versions of Python 2.7 older than this have this problem. Python 2.6 users are unaffected because since `argparse` isn't in the standard library, we use the `argparse` from PyPI which is a newer version of the module.

It appears that Debian 7.0 Wheezy is the only distro that both has a version of Python with this problem and is still supported by the distro maintainers. We have a lot of users on this platform though so I'll figure out a way to work around this problem.

Correct. `certbot renew` checks if you need to renew your certificates and if not, exits without doing anything else. Since you've included `--quiet` on the command line, it will only show error output which there probably isn't any. Try running:
```
sudo /usr/bin/certbot renew --post-hook "service nginx start"
````
This is the same command with `sh` and `--quiet` removed. You probably still want to include `--quiet` in your crontab though.

snakemake take a [very simple approach](https://bitbucket.org/snakemake/snakemake/src/096f56aaf6ced2614ee29207b56abe22eab05518/snakemake/executors.py?at=master&fileviewer=file-view-default#executors.py-483) that does not need any third-party library. Without first-hand experience, it seems that it requires users to login to the head node to run snakemake. Snakemake will wrap steps into bash scripts, submit jobs, and wait indefinitely for the completion of the jobs (there is a "progress" mechanism to monitor the jobs). This is not idea at least to us because we do not allow hanging jobs on the head nodes.

A non-invasive snakemake-like approach would be adding data synchronization functions/options (e.g. `remote(files, carrier=CONFIG['carrier_cmd'])`) and use ssh commands (e.g. `ssh cluster qsub < local_script`) to submit and monitor jobs. It is relatively straightforward (but not easy) to implement but I am not sure how well it would work.
We might also need to consider systems without `rsync`, for which `scp` has to be used. This is what `send_cmd` etc are for.

Currently, when on object is JSON encoded for a snapshot, if it does not belong to a hardcoded list of encodable objects, its string representation is saved instead. However, if you want to include a snapshot of a custom object, you may want certain attributes to be saved as well. As an example, I am using a PulseSequence object, which itself is composed of Pulse objects. I don't want to only save a string representation of the PulseSequence, but also want it to save all the pulses and their attributes, such as amplitude, frequency, start time, etc.

Hey @sigurdurb I think we have the solution to your problem! As @jessemcbride described above, the upload function works but to complete the process you need to attach the file via `Course.update_submission()`.

We found an issue with the `update_submission` method that prevented this attachment from working properly. We have since fixed it in #141.

With both the upload and update methods fixed, attaching is now possible!

```python
canvas = Canvas(API_URL, API_KEY)

course = canvas.get_course(COURSE_ID)
submission = course.get_submission(ASSIGNMENT_ID, USER_ID)
uploaded_file = submission.upload_comment(FILE_PATH)[1]
file_id = uploaded_file['id']
response = course.update_submission(
    ASSIGNMENT_ID,
    USER_ID,
    comment={
        'file_ids': [file_id]
    }
)
```

The above works, but it's not the most intuitive. To improve this process, we will investigate having the `upload_comment` method automatically link the file to the submission. I will open a separate issue for this.

Additionally, the `update_submission` method may change in the future, depending on how we handle #57. Keep an eye on the release notes of future patches for deprecation warnings.

Hi @ducha-aiki 
Sorry for the delay in replying.

I think extending the scope of the models in torchvision is good, but I have a few remarks about that that I'd like to share:
- having the training code used to obtain the model is important. You already open-sourced your implementation, so this is not a problem. But
  - given that the code is in a repo that does not live under pytorch org, I wonder once we have new releases of pytorch that have breaking changes, how we would do to keep things synchronized.
  - if the model contain 3rdparty modules, like [L2Norm](https://github.com/DagnyT/hardnet/blob/master/code/HardNet.py#L318), in order to run the code we might need your repo installed (in this case, we could replace this function with pytorch functions, but the same applies for custom losses). A solution could be to push the custom modules to pytorch / torchvision, but we need to figure out the right format for that.
- Once we figure those points out, I think we might want to have an hierarchical / task-based modelzoo, which will contain different models for a given task, and the training / evaluation code is easily available for the model. For the moment, the only (implicit) task we have is imagenet classification.

I'm open to suggestions on both points.

The tasks with status 8 are just dummy tasks for drivers.

In the case I am debugging right now, `entry.task_counter` is 0 (meaning we haven't executed any actor tasks yet), and `next_task_counter` is 1 (meaning that the first queued task is task 1). I printed out the queued tasks, and they have actor counters 1, 2, 3, ..., 80, but we are waiting for task 0.

Note that I have also seen situations where the queued tasks are more like 20, 21, 22, ..., 80, but we are waiting for task 0.

Task 0 is in state 32 (reconstructing). I've checked, and it looks like we are never calling `RAY.TASK_TABLE_TEST_AND_UPDATE` on task 0. We are repeatedly calling `RAY.TASK_TABLE_TEST_AND_UPDATE` on task 1, but it is getting suppressed because task 1 is QUEUED.

hi, sorry to bump a closed issue.. but this touches on the exact topic that I was hoping to solve..

I've been playing around with an old Chromecast lately.. brought out of retirement because I've modified a [Chrome extension](https://github.com/warren-bank/crx-webcast-reloaded) and been having great success in being able to push nearly every random video available on websites to the Chromecast with a few mouse clicks.

The issue I wanted to [solve](https://github.com/warren-bank/HLS-proxy) was when an .m3u8 and its .ts chunks are protected (weakly) by restrictive CORS headers. Meaning, a web browser (including the one embedded in Chromecast) will refuse to download content from a server that includes a header response that says the content can only be played on a particular website. This proxy tries to solve that by downloading the .m3u8 and rewriting all of its internal URLs inflight so they point back to the proxy.. and it also injects very permissive CORS headers. It works well.. but I noticed that maybe Streamlink could be used in a similar way..

I chose an .m3u8 randomly from the wild.. one that doesn't use CORS headers, and started a Streamlink server:
`streamlink --player-external-http --player-external-http-port 8080 --default-stream best --http-ignore-env --http-no-ssl-verify --url "https://XXX/video.m3u8"`

It seems to work great.. it shows client connections and responds with a data stream. The only problem is.. no clients seem to understand what the data stream is sending.. and then close the connection after a few seconds.

Specifically.. I tried:
* going to one of my [WebCast players](https://warren-bank.github.io/crx-webcast-reloaded/external_website/3-theoplayer/index.html)
* loading the video from [Streamlink](http://192.168.XXX.XXX:8080/)
  * when that failed, I also tried adding a [path that ends with a file extension](http://192.168.XXX.XXX:8080/video.m3u8), but same result

So, my questions would be:
* what is the format of the video being served by `--player-external-http`?
  * incidentally, adding `--player-passthrough "hls"` causes Streamlink to crash in the process of starting the server
* any ideas how this video stream could be played in a native HTML5 video tag?

Thanks all :)

Hello I propose this fix for this problem.

https://gist.github.com/Thordin/c7442eb21839543e073ce9f2ca53dfd4

Any datetime with no timezone will be considered localtime and then changed to UTC when inserting into clickhouse.

When you select datetime from database, it will be converted from UTC to the client's local timezone.

This way you will get the same datetime as you inserted. If your datetime is in a different timezone, you can set the timezone and it will be saved as UTC correctly.

```python
# setting timezone for now()
datetime.datetime.now(tz=some_time_zone)

# add timezone to datetime with no tz info
dt.replace(tzinfo=some_time_zone)
```

This fix will require the library dateutils. There is no other way to get local timezone without another library.

You can maybe add an option to change the local timezone to something different from the client.

I was trying to follow the logic in the validators for what to do in cases where there is a conflict. How does this sound for a design? The assumptions here are that the value is the most likely to change, the min/max second, and the min gap is the least likely to change.

* **Setting min gap**: setting a min gap that is too big (either bigger than value range or max range) fails with an error.
* **Setting min/max**: If this makes the max range smaller than min gap, fail with error. Otherwise, adjust the value to make sure the value is within the min/max.
* **Setting the value in python**: If the values are outside the min/max, clip them. Then check to see if the value_range is larger than the min_gap. If it is, great. If not, fail with error.
* **Setting the value in js**: If the values are outside the min/max, clip them (this could happen if setting the values from the readout). Then check to see if the value range is larger than min gap. If it is, great. If not, reset to old value.

What's happening here is that the matplotlib inline backend makes some assumptions about how to display graphs that don't mesh well with capturing output. We had to work around this in interact as well at https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/02f406bd3ce1480ed3df2533f6acc1a4587b52af/ipywidgets/widgets/interaction.py#L75, which calls this function: https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/02f406bd3ce1480ed3df2533f6acc1a4587b52af/ipywidgets/widgets/interaction.py#L35

So you can use the same workaround we used. Import that function:

```python
from ipywidgets.widgets.interaction import show_inline_matplotlib_plots
```

then change your function to:
```python
def on_button_clicked(b):
    with out:
        clear_output()
        x = pd.DataFrame(np.random.randn(8, 1))
        display(x.T)
        x.plot(figsize=[8, 1])
        show_inline_matplotlib_plots()
```
Even better would be to use the matplotlib widget, which lets you update a matplotlib graph, respond to user input, etc: https://github.com/matplotlib/jupyter-matplotlib
Closing in deference to the google doc, which will be easier to keep up to date: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lr3GQLRW2xq7oKbu7bpWQqWH2OcRBMx3DkVwTxHYmAU/edit#

Indeed, if you are using the static version of your metamodel, there is a solution. This solution is almost equivalent to the one for derived attributes. In your generated `.py` where the whole metamodel code is located, you can change this line (I suppose your code look like the following):

```python
# before
class NETWORK(EObject, metaclass=EMetaEClass):
    nWPACKAGES = EReference(containment=True)  # and possibly other options here

# after
class NETWORK(EObject, metaclass=EMetaEClass):
    nWPACKAGES = EReference(name='nW-PACKAGES', containment=True)  # This mod is useful for XMI serialization

setattr(NETWORK, 'nw-PACKAGES', PACKAGES.nWPACKAGES)  # this mod if useful for XMI deserialization
```

This modification can also be made for `EAttribute` and `x..*` relationships.

To make things work you need to create a `ProjectJob` object:
```python
myPipelineJobs = myPipeline.jobs.list()
myJob = next((x for x in myPipelineJobs if x.name == 'sync-source:prod'), None)
# creating a lazy object to avoid a new API call to the server
usable_job = project.jobs.get(myJob.id, lazy=True)
usable_job.retry()
```

Hello @BancarelValentin,

This is the expected behavior. your `user` is a `ProjectMember`, which doesn't expose methods to access the user emails and keys. python-gitlab reproduces the Gitlab API: https://docs.gitlab.com/ce/api/members.html

If you want to access the emails and keys you need to build a `User` object (setting `lazy` to `True` avoids an API call):

```python
>>> project = projects.members.list()[0]
>>> user = project.members.list()[0]
>>> type(user)
<class 'gitlab.v4.objects.ProjectMember'>
>>> real_user = gl.users.get(user.id, lazy=True)
>>> type(real_user)
<class 'gitlab.v4.objects.User'>
>>> real_user.emails.list()
[]
>>> real_user.keys.list()
[]
```

The coordinate values in the footprint geometries are perfectly correct in this case. The issue is more related to how your specific GIS software handles geometries that cross the dateline. One easy way to 'fix' this would be to use a different map projection, for example.
So I would not quite call this a bug, but making sure that none of the longitude values in a geometry do not wrap is a reasonable workaround that should work well for most GIS software and I'd say we should consider including this in `sentinelsat`.
